text: Simulate 500-subject virtual cohort whose weight distribution matches baseline demographics described Kielbasa 2020 Results (mean 75.8 kg, SD 16.8 kg, range 40-135.5 kg). paper publish individual-level weight distribution, sample normal distribution around reported mean SD, clipped reported range.
code: set.seed(2020) n_subj <- 500  pop <- tibble(   ID = seq_len(n_subj),   WT = pmin(pmax(rnorm(n_subj, mean = 75.8, sd = 16.8), 40), 135.5) )

title: Kielbasa_2020_galcanezumab
text: Replicate two phase 3 therapeutic regimens reported Kielbasa 2020 Results (PK Model Application): 120 mg SC monthly 240 mg loading dose month 0, 240 mg SC monthly. Simulate 12 months daily sampling first cycle weekly sampling thereafter.
code: obs_times <- sort(unique(c(   seq(0, 28, by = 1),   seq(28, 364, by = 7) )))  build_events <- function(pop, loading_mg, maint_mg, dose_times, regimen_label) {   dose_amts <- c(loading_mg, rep(maint_mg, length(dose_times) - 1L))   dose_rows <- pop %>%     tidyr::crossing(idx = seq_along(dose_times)) %>%     mutate(       time = dose_times[idx],       amt  = dose_amts[idx],       evid = 1,       cmt  = "depot",       dv   = NA_real_     ) %>%     select(-idx)   obs_rows <- pop %>%     tidyr::crossing(time = obs_times) %>%     mutate(amt = NA_real_, evid = 0, cmt = NA_character_, dv = NA_real_)   bind_rows(dose_rows, obs_rows) %>%     mutate(treatment = regimen_label) %>%     arrange(ID, time, desc(evid)) }  events_ld120 <- build_events(pop,   loading_mg    = 240,   maint_mg      = 120,   dose_times    = seq(0, by = 28, length.out = 12),   regimen_label = "240 mg LD + 120 mg QM")  events_240qm <- build_events(pop,   loading_mg    = 240,   maint_mg      = 240,   dose_times    = seq(0, by = 28, length.out = 12),   regimen_label = "240 mg QM")  events_all <- bind_rows(events_ld120, events_240qm)

title: Kielbasa_2020_galcanezumab
code: mod <- readModelDb("Kielbasa_2020_galcanezumab")  sim <- events_all %>%   group_by(treatment) %>%   group_modify(~ {     ev <- .x %>% rename(id = ID)     as_tibble(rxSolve(mod, ev, returnType = "data.frame"))   }) %>%   ungroup() #> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()' #> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()'

what: Replicate Figure 3: concentration-time profiles with and without loading dose
title: Kielbasa_2020_galcanezumab
text: Figure 3 Kielbasa 2020 highlights 240 mg loading dose roughly doubles galcanezumab concentrations month 0 month 1 compared 120 mg QM without loading dose, 120 mg QM alone takes 4-5 months reach steady state.
code: fig3 <- sim %>%   filter(time > 0) %>%   group_by(treatment, time) %>%   summarise(     median = median(Cc, na.rm = TRUE),     lo     = quantile(Cc, 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),     hi     = quantile(Cc, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   )  ggplot(fig3, aes(x = time / 7)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lo, ymax = hi, fill = treatment), alpha = 0.2) +   geom_line(aes(y = median, colour = treatment), linewidth = 1) +   facet_wrap(~treatment) +   labs(     x       = "Time since first dose (weeks)",     y       = "Galcanezumab concentration (ug/mL)",     title   = "Figure 3 replication - simulated galcanezumab exposure",     caption = "Replicates Figure 3 of Kielbasa 2020. Median and 90% prediction interval (N = 500)."   ) +   theme_bw() +   theme(legend.position = "none")

what: Replicate Figure 4: effect of body weight on steady-state exposure
title: Kielbasa_2020_galcanezumab
text: Figure 4 Kielbasa 2020 shows galcanezumab concentration-time profiles 5th (52 kg) 95th (105 kg) percentiles body weight 240 mg LD + 120 mg QM regimen, lower median concentrations heaviest patients substantial overlap 90% prediction interval.
code: pop_bw_extremes <- tibble(   ID = seq_len(1000),   WT = c(rep(52, 500), rep(105, 500)),   wt_band = c(rep("5th percentile (52 kg)", 500),               rep("95th percentile (105 kg)", 500)) )  events_bw <- build_events(pop_bw_extremes,   loading_mg    = 240,   maint_mg      = 120,   dose_times    = seq(0, by = 28, length.out = 12),   regimen_label = "240 mg LD + 120 mg QM") %>%   mutate(wt_band = pop_bw_extremes$wt_band[match(ID, pop_bw_extremes$ID)])  sim_bw <- rxSolve(mod, events_bw %>% rename(id = ID),                   returnType = "data.frame",                   keep = "wt_band") %>%   as_tibble() #> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()'  fig4 <- sim_bw %>%   filter(time > 0) %>%   group_by(wt_band, time) %>%   summarise(     median = median(Cc, na.rm = TRUE),     lo     = quantile(Cc, 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),     hi     = quantile(Cc, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   )  ggplot(fig4, aes(x = time / 7, colour = wt_band, fill = wt_band)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lo, ymax = hi), alpha = 0.15, colour = NA) +   geom_line(aes(y = median), linewidth = 1) +   labs(     x       = "Time since first dose (weeks)",     y       = "Galcanezumab concentration (ug/mL)",     colour  = "Body weight",     fill    = "Body weight",     title   = "Figure 4 replication - 240 mg LD + 120 mg QM by body weight",     caption = "Replicates lower panel of Figure 4 of Kielbasa 2020. Median and 90% prediction interval (N = 500 per band)."   ) +   theme_bw() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Kielbasa_2020_galcanezumab
text: Paper-reported PK metrics simulation can compared (Kielbasa 2020 Results, PK Model Application): apparent terminal elimination half-life: 27 days; median Tmax subcutaneous dosing: 5 days; Cmin 240 mg loading dose: 15,900 ng/mL (= 15.9 ug/mL); Cmin,ss 120 mg QM (240 mg loading dose): 15,400 ng/mL (= 15.4 ug/mL). Compute simulated Cmax, Tmax, terminal half-life first dosing interval 240 mg LD + 120 mg QM regimen. PKNCA grouped treatment + id. Simulated NCA first dosing interval 240 mg LD + 120 mg QM (N = 500). Typical-value terminal half-life derived structural parameters vs. Kielbasa 2020 reported value. Cmin comparison: simulated medians vs. Kielbasa 2020 reported values. simulated Cmin values terminal half-life within approximately 20% published values. Larger deviations motivate re-examination dose schedule unit conversions rather parameter tuning.
code: nca_conc <- sim %>%   filter(treatment == "240 mg LD + 120 mg QM",          time <= 28,          !is.na(Cc), Cc > 0) %>%   transmute(id, time, Cc, treatment = "240 mg LD (interval 1)", WT)  nca_dose <- nca_conc %>%   distinct(id, treatment, WT) %>%   mutate(time = 0, amt = 240) conc_obj <- PKNCAconc(nca_conc, Cc ~ time | treatment + id,                       concu = "ug/mL", timeu = "day") dose_obj <- PKNCAdose(nca_dose, amt ~ time | treatment + id,                       doseu = "mg")  intervals <- data.frame(   start     = 0,   end       = 28,   cmax      = TRUE,   tmax      = TRUE,   cmin      = TRUE,   auclast   = TRUE )  nca_data <- PKNCAdata(conc_obj, dose_obj, intervals = intervals) nca_res  <- pk.nca(nca_data)  nca_tbl <- as.data.frame(nca_res$result) %>%   filter(PPTESTCD %in% c("cmax", "tmax", "cmin", "auclast")) %>%   group_by(PPTESTCD) %>%   summarise(median = median(PPORRES, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")  knitr::kable(nca_tbl,              digits  = 3,              caption = "Simulated NCA on the first dosing interval of 240 mg LD + 120 mg QM (N = 500).") # Typical-value terminal half-life from the packaged structural parameters # (CL/F = 0.1884 L/day, V/F = 7.33 L at the 73.6 kg reference). typ_cl   <- 0.1884 typ_vc   <- 7.33 typ_kel  <- typ_cl / typ_vc typ_half <- log(2) / typ_kel  data.frame(   metric = c("CL/F at 73.6 kg (L/day)",              "V/F (L)",              "kel (1/day)",              "half-life (day)",              "published half-life (day)"),   value  = signif(c(typ_cl, typ_vc, typ_kel, typ_half, 27), 4) ) %>%   knitr::kable(caption = "Typical-value terminal half-life derived from the structural parameters vs. Kielbasa 2020 reported value.") cmin_sim <- sim %>%   filter(treatment == "240 mg LD + 120 mg QM") %>%   mutate(month = floor(time / 28)) %>%   group_by(id, month) %>%   summarise(cmin_interval = min(Cc, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%   group_by(month) %>%   summarise(median_cmin_ugmL = median(cmin_interval, na.rm = TRUE),             .groups = "drop")  cmin_table <- tibble(   metric    = c("Cmin after 240 mg LD (end of month 1)",                 "Cmin,ss during 120 mg QM (end of month 11)"),   published_ngmL = c(15900, 15400),   simulated_ugmL = c(cmin_sim$median_cmin_ugmL[cmin_sim$month == 1],                      cmin_sim$median_cmin_ugmL[cmin_sim$month == 11]), ) %>%   mutate(simulated_ngmL = simulated_ugmL * 1000,          ratio_sim_pub  = simulated_ngmL / published_ngmL)  knitr::kable(cmin_table,              digits  = 2,              caption = "Cmin comparison: simulated medians vs. Kielbasa 2020 reported values.")

title: Kielbasa_2020_galcanezumab
text: Weight distribution. Kielbasa 2020 publish per-subject weight distribution. sample normal distribution mean 75.8 kg SD 16.8 kg (matching reported summary statistics), clipped reported 40-135.5 kg range. Time base converted hours days. source paper reports ka 1/h CL/F L/h; packaged model converts 1/day L/day (ka = 0.0199 * 24 = 0.4776 /day, CL/F = 0.00785 * 24 = 0.1884 L/day) time unit matches mAb models nlmixr2lib (mAbs 27-day half-life naturally simulate daily grid). reparameterization mathematically identical; V/F unchanged. ka-V/F covariance fixed 0. Kielbasa 2020 Table 3 reports covariances (ka, CL/F) (CL/F, V/F); third -diagonal estimated. packaged OMEGA block carries explicit zero cov(ka, V/F) preserve positive-definiteness. Body weight time-invariant baseline covariate. source paper state whether body weight enters covariate model baseline time-varying. simulate WT subject-level baseline. Injection site, age, sex, race, ethnicity, ADA status, creatinine clearance, bilirubin tested covariates source paper retained final model (Results, PK Model Development). consequently absent packaged model. External validation study (I5Q-MC-CGAJ) used source paper held-validation dataset. packaged model uses published final-model parameter values, estimated development dataset .

title: Kielbasa_2020_galcanezumab
text: Structural model: 1-compartment first-order SC absorption linear elimination. Apparent clearance scales allometrically body weight via power model (exponent 0.601, reference 73.6 kg). IIV: full 3x3 OMEGA block ka, CL/F, V/F (ka, V/F) -diagonal element held zero. Residual error: proportional 22% CV. Terminal half-life predicted structural parameters approximately 27 days, matching paper’s reported value.

title: Kielbasa_2020_galcanezumab
text: Kielbasa W, Quinlan T. Population Pharmacokinetics Galcanezumab, Anti-CGRP Antibody, Following Subcutaneous Dosing Healthy Individuals Patients Migraine. J Clin Pharmacol. 2020;60(2):229-239. doi:10.1002/jcph.1511

title: Koopman_2023_factorix
text: Citation: Koopman SF, Goedhart TMHJ, Bukkems LH, et al. new population pharmacokinetic model recombinant factor IX-Fc fusion concentrate including young children haemophilia B. Br J Clin Pharmacol. 2024;90(1):220-231. doi:[10.1111/bcp.15881](https://doi.org/10.1111/bcp.15881) Description: Two-compartment population PK model recombinant factor IX-Fc fusion protein (rFIX-Fc, eftrenonacog alfa, Alprolix) haemophilia B patients aged 2-71 years. Article: https://doi.org/10.1111/bcp.15881 Modality: Fc-fusion factor concentrate, IV bolus. rFIX-Fc extended half-life FIX concentrate consisting single recombinant factor IX molecule fused dimeric Fc domain human IgG1. Fc domain binds FcRn, delaying lysosomal degradation prolonging circulation half-life relative standard rFIX. Koopman 2023 externally evaluated previously published rFIX-Fc population PK model (Diao 2014, based patients aged >= 12 years) using real-world data found systematic underprediction (median PE -48.8% across patients, -54.1% children <12 years). developed new two-compartment model fit combined OPTI-CLOT TARGET (Netherlands) UK-EHL Outcomes Registry cohorts. packaged model implements new Koopman 2023 model (right-hand column Koopman 2023 Table 2; equations p. 226), published Diao 2014 model. new model single covariate effect: linear-deviation slope age CL, centered cohort median age 15.8 years. Allometric scaling PK parameters body weight (reference 73 kg) applied theoretical exponents (0.75 CL Q; 1.00 V1 V2).

title: Koopman_2023_factorix
text: development cohort comprised 37 patients haemophilia B treated prophylactically rFIX-Fc (Koopman 2023 Table 1): 35 severe (FIX < 1 IU/dL) 2 moderately severe haemophilia B. Age 2-71 years (median 15.8 years, IQR 11-30). Body weight 12-103 kg (median 65.4 kg, IQR 33-77). 19/37 (51%) paediatric (< 18 years), 14/37 (38%) < 12 years, 7/37 (19%) < 6 years. Median dose 36 IU/kg rFIX-Fc IV (range 10-132 IU/kg). Median 5 FIX activity samples per PK profile (range 3-7); 287 measurements total, 3 LLOQ excluded. Sex: hemophilia B X-linked, cohort essentially male. Region: Netherlands (OPTI-CLOT TARGET, NTR7523) United Kingdom (UK-EHL Outcomes Registry, NCT02938156). metadata available programmatically via readModelDb("Koopman_2023_factorix")$population.

title: Koopman_2023_factorix
text: per-parameter origin recorded -file comment next ini() entry inst/modeldb/specificDrugs/Koopman_2023_factorix.R. table collects one place review. footnote Koopman 2023 Table 2 states “IIV IOV coefficient variation calculated : sqrt(variance)*100%”, .e., reported CV% values sqrt(omega^2) * 100, omega^2 = (CV/100)^2. IIV variances computed squaring 0.236, 0.316, 0.412, CL:V1 covariance 0.44 * 0.236 * 0.316. new model also reports inter-occasion variability CL (IOV CL = 19.8%) implemented static model file; see Assumptions deviations.

title: Koopman_2023_factorix
text: Original observed FIX activity data publicly available. simulations use virtual cohort whose demographics approximate Koopman 2023 development population, stratified three age groups used paper’s half-life table (Supplementary Table 3): children < 12 years, adolescents 12 - < 18 years, adults >= 18 years. Body weight sampled log-normal distribution centered typical weight--age within band, capped observed range Koopman 2023 Table 1. single 50 IU/kg IV bolus dose rFIX-Fc administered time 0 FIX activity observed 14-day window (typical clinical follow-Q1W prophylaxis doses).
code: set.seed(2023)  make_cohort <- function(n, age_min, age_max, wt_log_mean, wt_log_sd,                         wt_lo, wt_hi, label, id_offset = 0L) {   tibble(     ID        = id_offset + seq_len(n),     AGE       = runif(n, age_min, age_max),     WT        = pmin(pmax(rlnorm(n, log(wt_log_mean), wt_log_sd), wt_lo), wt_hi),     age_group = label   ) }  n_per_group <- 100L cohort <- bind_rows(   make_cohort(n_per_group, 2,  12, 22, 0.30, 12, 45,               "Children <12 y",         id_offset = 0L),   make_cohort(n_per_group, 12, 18, 50, 0.20, 35, 80,               "Adolescents 12-<18 y",   id_offset = 100L),   # Adults are sampled over 18-50 to approximate the median age of the   # development cohort (overall median 15.8 y, paediatric n = 19, adult   # n = 18, so the adult median is likely in the late twenties / early   # thirties — a uniform 18-71 sample shifts the median half-life upward   # because of the linear age-on-CL effect).   make_cohort(n_per_group, 18, 50, 75, 0.20, 50, 110,               "Adults >=18 y",          id_offset = 200L) )  stopifnot(!anyDuplicated(cohort$ID)) summary(cohort) #>        ID              AGE               WT             age_group   #>  Min.   :  1.00   Min.   : 2.304   Min.   : 12.00   Length   :300   #>  1st Qu.: 75.75   1st Qu.: 9.572   1st Qu.: 27.14   N.unique :  3   #>  Median :150.50   Median :15.362   Median : 49.49   N.blank  :  0   #>  Mean   :150.50   Mean   :18.746   Mean   : 49.23   Min.nchar: 13   #>  3rd Qu.:225.25   3rd Qu.:27.273   3rd Qu.: 66.34   Max.nchar: 20   #>  Max.   :300.00   Max.   :50.000   Max.   :110.00 obs_grid <- c(0, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 24,               seq(48, 336, by = 12))  build_events <- function(pop) {   amt_per_subject <- pop$WT * 50   d_dose <- pop |>     mutate(AMT = amt_per_subject) |>     tidyr::crossing(TIME = 0) |>     mutate(EVID = 1, CMT = "central", DV = NA_real_)   d_obs <- pop |>     tidyr::crossing(TIME = obs_grid) |>     mutate(AMT = NA_real_, EVID = 0, CMT = "central", DV = NA_real_)   bind_rows(d_dose, d_obs) |>     arrange(ID, TIME, desc(EVID)) |>     as.data.frame() }  events <- build_events(cohort)

title: Koopman_2023_factorix
text: Run stochastic VPC-style simulation (-subject variability CL, V1, V2 included) typical-value simulation etas zeroed direct parameter back-checks.
code: mod <- readModelDb("Koopman_2023_factorix")  sim <- rxode2::rxSolve(mod, events = events,                        keep = c("age_group"),                        returnType = "data.frame") #> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()' sim <- sim[sim$time >= 0, ]  mod_typ <- rxode2::zeroRe(mod) #> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()' sim_typ <- rxode2::rxSolve(mod_typ, events = events,                            keep = c("age_group"),                            returnType = "data.frame") #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalcl', 'etalvc', 'etalvp' #> Warning: multi-subject simulation without without 'omega' sim_typ <- sim_typ[sim_typ$time >= 0, ]

what: FIX activity-time profiles by age group
title: Koopman_2023_factorix
text: Koopman 2023 Figure 3 shows visual predictive check new model across development cohort. figure reproduces typical-value median + 5 - 95% prediction interval FIX activity vs. time dose, stratified three age bands used paper’s half-life summary (Supplementary Table 3). later times paediatric profile sits adult profile () typical V1 scales linearly WT, per-dose Cmax similar across age groups (Cmax = dose / V1 = (50 * WT) / (73.1 * WT/73), WT-independent), (b) typical CL/V1 (apparent first-order elimination rate constant central compartment) higher low WT allometric scaling exponent 0.75 / 1.0 (CL/V1 scales WT^(-0.25)), trough activity lower terminal half-life shorter younger / smaller patients.
code: sim_summary <- sim |>   filter(time > 0) |>   group_by(time, age_group) |>   summarise(     median = stats::median(Cc, na.rm = TRUE),     lo     = stats::quantile(Cc, 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),     hi     = stats::quantile(Cc, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   )  ggplot(sim_summary, aes(time, median, colour = age_group, fill = age_group)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lo, ymax = hi), alpha = 0.18, colour = NA) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1) +   scale_y_log10() +   labs(     x        = "Time after dose (h)",     y        = "FIX activity above baseline (IU/dL, log scale)",     colour   = "Age group",     fill     = "Age group",     title    = "Simulated rFIX-Fc PK after a single 50 IU/kg IV dose",     subtitle = paste0("Median + 5-95% prediction interval (N = ", n_per_group,                       " per group). Approximate analogue of Koopman 2023 Figure 3."),     caption  = "Model: Koopman 2023 Br J Clin Pharmacol 90(1):220-231"   ) +   theme_bw()

title: Koopman_2023_factorix
text: Koopman 2023 reports typical 16-year-old, 73 kg patient (“reference” subject new model), CL = 1.41 dL/h steady-state volume distribution Vss = V1 + V2 = 73.1 + 80.1 = 153 dL. Reproducing numbers typical-value simulation simplest possible self-consistency check. Typical-value PK parameters reference 73 kg, 15.8-year-old patient model returns CL = 1.41 dL/h, V1 = 73.1 dL, Q2 = 2.77 dL/h, V2 = 80.1 dL, Vss = V1 + V2 = 153.2 dL — matching values reported Koopman 2023 Table 2 (CL = 1.41 dL/h, V1 = 73.1 dL, Q2 = 2.77 dL/h, V2 = 80.1 dL, Vss = 153 dL).
code: mod_typ <- rxode2::zeroRe(mod) #> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()' ev_ref <- rxode2::et(amt = 50 * 73, time = 0, cmt = "central") |>   rxode2::et(seq(0, 0)) sim_ref <- rxode2::rxSolve(   mod_typ, events = ev_ref,   params = data.frame(WT = 73, AGE = 15.8),   returnType = "data.frame" ) #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalcl', 'etalvc', 'etalvp'  # rxSolve carries the derived individual parameters as columns when the model # defines them (cl, vc, q, vp). We pull them out of the first observation row. ref_pars <- sim_ref[1, c("cl", "vc", "q", "vp"), drop = FALSE] ref_pars$Vss <- ref_pars$vc + ref_pars$vp  knitr::kable(   ref_pars,   caption = "Typical-value PK parameters for the reference 73 kg, 15.8-year-old patient",   digits  = c(3, 2, 3, 2, 2) )

title: Koopman_2023_factorix
text: Use PKNCA compute Cmax, AUC0-inf terminal half-life age group, compare simulated half-lives Koopman 2023 Supplementary Table 3. Simulated rFIX-Fc terminal half-life (h) age group, single 50 IU/kg IV dose.
code: sim_nca <- sim |>   filter(!is.na(Cc), Cc > 0) |>   select(id, time, Cc, age_group)  dose_df <- events |>   filter(EVID == 1) |>   transmute(id = ID, time = TIME, amt = AMT, age_group)  conc_obj <- PKNCA::PKNCAconc(sim_nca, Cc ~ time | age_group + id,                              concu = "IU/dL",                              timeu = "h") dose_obj <- PKNCA::PKNCAdose(dose_df, amt ~ time | age_group + id,                              doseu = "IU")  intervals <- data.frame(   start      = 0,   end        = Inf,   cmax       = TRUE,   tmax       = TRUE,   aucinf.obs = TRUE,   half.life  = TRUE,   clast.obs  = TRUE,   lambda.z   = TRUE )  nca_res <- PKNCA::pk.nca(PKNCA::PKNCAdata(conc_obj, dose_obj,                                           intervals = intervals)) #>  ■■■■                              11% |  ETA: 10s #>  ■■■■■■■■■■■■■                     41% |  ETA:  6s #>  ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■            71% |  ETA:  3s #>  ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■  100% |  ETA:  0s nca_tbl <- as.data.frame(nca_res$result)  half_life_summary <- nca_tbl |>   filter(PPTESTCD == "half.life") |>   group_by(age_group) |>   summarise(     n        = sum(!is.na(PPORRES)),     median_h = stats::median(PPORRES, na.rm = TRUE),     q05      = stats::quantile(PPORRES, 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),     q95      = stats::quantile(PPORRES, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups  = "drop"   )  knitr::kable(   half_life_summary,   caption = "Simulated rFIX-Fc terminal half-life (h) by age group, single 50 IU/kg IV dose.",   digits  = c(0, 0, 1, 1, 1) )

title: Koopman_2023_factorix
text: Koopman 2023 Supplementary Table 3 reports post-hoc terminal half-lives new model three age bands. simulated medians fall within reported observed ranges; differences > 20% reported median indicate coding problem. Simulated vs. Koopman 2023 Supplementary Table 3 terminal half-lives. typical Cmax check: typical-value simulation gives Cmax every age group (50 * WT / (73.1 * WT / 73) = 49.93 IU/dL), expected linear allometric scaling exponent 1.0 V1.
code: published <- tibble::tribble(   ~age_group,                 ~published_median_h, ~published_range,   "Children <12 y",           70,                  "51-103",   "Adolescents 12-<18 y",     76,                  "66-82",   "Adults >=18 y",            88,                  "67-166" )  comparison <- published |>   left_join(     half_life_summary |>       select(age_group, simulated_median_h = median_h),     by = "age_group"   ) |>   mutate(     pct_diff = round(100 * (simulated_median_h - published_median_h) /                        published_median_h, 1)   )  knitr::kable(   comparison,   caption = "Simulated vs. Koopman 2023 Supplementary Table 3 terminal half-lives.",   digits  = c(0, 0, 0, 1, 1) )

title: Koopman_2023_factorix
text: published erratum located Koopman 2023 (Br J Clin Pharmacol 2024;90(1):220-231). packaged parameter values taken Koopman 2023 Table 2 (“New” column) equations p. 226, internally consistent: CL = 1.41 dL/h typical CL reference covariates WT = 73 kg AGE = 15.8 years, Vss = V1 + V2 = 73.1 + 80.1 = 153 dL matches discussion text (“distribution volume steady-state also lower respective values 153 198 dL”). One narrative passage page 225 appears inconsistent equation page 226 Table 2: “basis relationship, typical clearance 73-kg patient decrease 1.89 dL/h age 20 years 1.36 dL/h 70 years.” Substituting (WT = 73, AGE = 20, AGE = 70) published equation CL = 1.41 * (73/73)^0.75 * (1 - 0.0047 * (AGE - 15.8)) gives 1.38 dL/h age 20 1.05 dL/h age 70 - direction (CL decreases age) ~25-35% lower absolute magnitude values quoted discussion. packaged model uses Table 2 values published equation authoritative, since internally consistent reproduce half-life table (Supplementary Table 3) within reported observed ranges.

title: Koopman_2023_factorix
text: Inter-occasion variability omitted. Koopman 2023 estimated IOV CL (19.8% CV, RSE 22%, shrinkage 36%) reflecting within-subject variability across PK profiling visits. static library model occasion variable, IOV implemented separate eta. Bayesian forecasting use cases explicitly model occasions, IOV variance (omega^2_IOV_CL = 0.198^2 = 0.03920) added top packaged IIV. Inter-individual variability convention. Koopman 2023 footnotes Table 2 “IIV IOV coefficient variation calculated : sqrt(variance) * 100%”, .e., reported CV% values sqrt(omega^2) * 100, log-normal sqrt(exp(omega^2) - 1). packaged variances computed simpler omega^2 = (CV/100)^2 formula match paper’s convention. IIV Q2. Koopman 2023 Table 2 reports IIV CL, V1 V2; IIV estimated Q2. packaged model carries etalq. FIX activity baseline-corrected. one-stage FIX activity assay distinguish endogenous baseline FIX exogenous rFIX-Fc, Koopman 2023 corrects observations endogenous baseline residual decay prior factor product (Equations 1 - 3) fitting. packaged model output Cc represents rFIX-Fc-attributable FIX activity baseline, IU/dL. Allometric exponents fixed theoretical values. Koopman 2023 fixed body weight exponents 0.75 (CL, Q) 1.00 (V1, V2) per Anderson & Holford 2008, rather estimating . packaged model() block hard-codes constants accordingly. Reference age centering. age effect centered 15.8 years (median age development cohort). Setting AGE = 15.8 recovers typical-value parameters Table 2; setting AGE outside studied range (2 - 71 years) extrapolates linear age slope without empirical support. Virtual cohort. Demographics simulated span three age bands used Koopman 2023 Supplementary Table 3 (children < 12 y, adolescents 12 - < 18 y, adults >= 18 y) weight--age sampled log-normal distribution capped observed weight range Koopman 2023 Table 1 (12-103 kg). Joint covariate structure (e.g., narrow weight range within pediatric age year) simulated. Number compartments. Koopman 2023 fit 2-compartment model noted rapid distribution phase (occurring within 2-3 h end administration, captured third compartment Diao 2014) resolvable sparser real-world sampling schedule. Cmax trough predictions sampling beyond ~4 h post-dose affected; users need model -early distribution phase consider Diao 2014 instead. Hemophilia B X-linked recessive, development cohort virtual cohort essentially male (sex_female_pct = 0 population metadata). model sex covariate.

title: Kotani_2022_astegolimab
text: Astegolimab anti-ST2 IgG2 monoclonal antibody developed severe asthma. Kotani et al. (2022) fit two-compartment population PK model first-order subcutaneous (SC) absorption first-order elimination Zenyatta (NCT02918019) Phase 2b data 368 adult patients. Structurally, model estimates apparent clearance (CL/F), central volume (Vc/F), peripheral volume (Vp/F), intercompartmental clearance (Q/F), absorption rate (ka). Shared allometric exponents body weight fit {CL, Q} {Vc, Vp}. Baseline CRCL (MDRD eGFR) baseline blood eosinophil count enter CL power covariates, binary indicator 70 mg dose arm scales relative bioavailability (Frel) −15.3% Box-Cox transformed IIV per Petersson et al. (2009). Citation: Kotani N, Dolton M, Svensson RJ, et al. Population Pharmacokinetics Exposure-Response Relationships Astegolimab Patients Severe Asthma. J Clin Pharmacol. 2022;62(7):905-917. doi:10.1002/jcph.2021 Article: https://doi.org/10.1002/jcph.2021

title: Kotani_2022_astegolimab
text: PK analysis data set comprises 368 adults severe asthma medium- high-dose inhaled corticosteroids plus least one additional controller least one exacerbation prior 12 months. Median baseline characteristics (Table 1): age 53 years (18–75), body weight 79 kg (43–130), CRCL 87.9 mL/min/1.73 m² (MDRD eGFR), blood eosinophil count 180 cells/µL. 66.8% female; 84.0% White, 5.4% Black, 4.6% Asian, 4.6% Native American, 1.4% multiple races. Treatment-emergent anti-drug antibodies observed 7.3% patients. Patients randomized 70, 210, 490 mg SC every 4 weeks (Q4W) 52 weeks. population metadata available programmatically via readModelDb("Kotani_2022_astegolimab")$population.

title: Kotani_2022_astegolimab
text: parameter values functional forms come Kotani et al. (2022) J Clin Pharmacol 62(7):905-917. -file comments next ini() entry record row-level source; table collects .

title: Kotani_2022_astegolimab
text: Individual-level Zenyatta data public. Simulate 450 virtual patients (150 per dose arm) whose covariate distributions approximate Table 1.
code: set.seed(2022) n_per_dose <- 150 doses <- c(70, 210, 490) n_subj <- n_per_dose * length(doses)  pop <- tibble(   ID        = seq_len(n_subj),   dose_mg   = rep(doses, each = n_per_dose),   # Body weight ~ lognormal matching median 79 kg and range 43-130 kg   WT        = pmin(130, pmax(43, rlnorm(n_subj, log(79), 0.22))),   # CRCL (MDRD eGFR) ~ normal matching median 87.9; clip to observed physiological range   CRCL      = pmin(160, pmax(30, rnorm(n_subj, 87.9, 20))),   # Blood eosinophils: right-skewed, median 180 cells/uL   EOS      = pmin(2000, pmax(5, rlnorm(n_subj, log(180), 0.9))),   DOSE_70MG = as.integer(dose_mg == 70) ) pop$treatment <- factor(   paste0(pop$dose_mg, " mg Q4W"),   levels = c("70 mg Q4W", "210 mg Q4W", "490 mg Q4W") )

title: Kotani_2022_astegolimab
text: Q4W dosing 52 weeks (14 doses). Weekly sampling week 60.
code: dose_times <- seq(0, by = 28, length.out = 14)      # days obs_times  <- sort(unique(c(   seq(0, 28, by = 3.5),   seq(28, 420, by = 7) )))  d_dose <- pop |>   tidyr::crossing(TIME = dose_times) |>   mutate(AMT = dose_mg, EVID = 1, CMT = "depot", DV = NA_real_)  d_obs <- pop |>   tidyr::crossing(TIME = obs_times) |>   mutate(AMT = 0, EVID = 0, CMT = "central", DV = NA_real_)  events <- bind_rows(d_dose, d_obs) |>   arrange(ID, TIME, desc(EVID)) |>   select(ID, TIME, AMT, EVID, CMT, DV,          WT, CRCL, EOS, DOSE_70MG, treatment, dose_mg)

title: Kotani_2022_astegolimab
code: mod <- readModelDb("Kotani_2022_astegolimab") sim <- rxode2::rxSolve(mod, events = events) #> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()'

what: Replicate published figure — concentration–time by dose group
title: Kotani_2022_astegolimab
text: Kotani et al. (2022) Figure 2 shows observed vs. model-predicted astegolimab concentrations time 70, 210, 490 mg SC Q4W. plot analogous VPC packaged model.
code: treatment_map <- pop |> select(ID, treatment)  sim_plot <- sim |>   as.data.frame() |>   filter(time > 0) |>   left_join(treatment_map, by = c("id" = "ID"))  vpc <- sim_plot |>   group_by(time, treatment) |>   summarise(     Q05 = quantile(Cc, 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),     Q50 = quantile(Cc, 0.50, na.rm = TRUE),     Q95 = quantile(Cc, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   )  ggplot(vpc, aes(time / 7, Q50)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = Q05, ymax = Q95), fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.25) +   geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.8) +   facet_wrap(~treatment) +   scale_y_log10() +   labs(     x = "Time (weeks)",     y = "Astegolimab serum concentration (\u00b5g/mL)",     title = "Astegolimab VPC by dose group (SC Q4W x 14 doses)",     caption = "Replicates the dose-stratified VPC panels in Kotani 2022 Figure 2."   ) +   theme_bw()

title: Kotani_2022_astegolimab
text: Compute NCA first dosing interval (days 0–28) using PKNCA dose group treatment grouping variable. Simulated NCA (0–28 day dosing interval) Zenyatta dose arm.
code: sim_nca <- sim |>   as.data.frame() |>   filter(!is.na(Cc), time >= 0, time <= 28) |>   left_join(treatment_map, by = c("id" = "ID")) |>   transmute(id = id, time = time, Cc = Cc, treatment = treatment)  dose_df <- events |>   filter(EVID == 1, TIME == 0) |>   transmute(id = ID, time = TIME, amt = AMT, treatment = treatment)  conc_obj <- PKNCA::PKNCAconc(sim_nca, Cc ~ time | treatment + id) dose_obj <- PKNCA::PKNCAdose(dose_df, amt ~ time | treatment + id)  intervals <- data.frame(   start    = 0,   end      = 28,   cmax     = TRUE,   tmax     = TRUE,   auclast  = TRUE )  nca_data <- PKNCA::PKNCAdata(conc_obj, dose_obj, intervals = intervals) nca_res  <- suppressWarnings(PKNCA::pk.nca(nca_data)) #>  ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■                    43% |  ETA:  2s nca_summary <- summary(nca_res) knitr::kable(   nca_summary,   caption = "Simulated NCA (0–28 day dosing interval) by Zenyatta dose arm." )

title: Kotani_2022_astegolimab
text: Kotani et al. report terminal half-life 19.6 days (Abstract / Results). model’s analytical λz computed disposition micro-constants (kel, k12, k21) smaller root characteristic equation.
code: CL <- 0.244; Vc <- 0.614; Q <- 0.171; Vp <- 2.74 kel <- CL / Vc; k12 <- Q / Vc; k21 <- Q / Vp s   <- kel + k12 + k21 lambda_z <- 0.5 * (s - sqrt(s^2 - 4 * kel * k21)) cat(sprintf("Analytical terminal t1/2 = %.2f days (published 19.6).\n",             log(2) / lambda_z)) #> Analytical terminal t1/2 = 19.65 days (published 19.6).

title: Kotani_2022_astegolimab
text: Kotani et al. tabulate NCA metrics per dose group, report terminal half-life (Abstract) state Discussion exposure dose-proportional 70–490 mg range adjusting 70 mg Frel offset. simulated median Cmax AUClast first dose scale dose × Frel (Frel = 0.847 70 mg arm, 1.00 210 490 mg arms). simulated absorption-phase half-life (≈16 days ka = 0.0437/day) dominates observed terminal slope single SC dose absorption slower disposition (flip-flop). Steady-state troughs therefore reached several months repeat Q4W dosing.

title: Kotani_2022_astegolimab
text: Covariate distributions. Approximated Table 1 aggregate statistics: weight ~ lognormal centered 79 kg median; CRCL ~ normal centered 87.9 mL/min/1.73 m²; blood eosinophils ~ lognormal centered 180 cells/µL. original subject-level joint distribution public. Dose allocation. 150 subjects per dose arm (70, 210, 490 mg) — balanced. Zenyatta randomized 2:1:1:1 placebo:70:210:490, active per-arm N ≈ 100 ; balanced synthetic cohort chosen VPC readability . Baseline-covariates. Kotani 2022 uses baseline WT / CRCL / blood eosinophils (time-fixed per subject). convention applied event table. ADA status. ADA reported 7.3% patients significant covariate astegolimab PK final model. simulation introduce ADA effect, matching paper. Residual error. Combined additive + proportional natural scale (Cc ~ add(0.603 ug/mL) + prop(0.198)), matching Table 2. IIV Frel. Box-Cox transformed η (shape −2.81) per Eq. 2; η variance square pre-Box-Cox SD reported Table 2 (0.243² = 0.05905).

title: Kovalenko_2016_dupilumab
text: Citation: Kovalenko P, DiCioccio , Davis JD, et al. Exploratory Population PK Analysis Dupilumab, Fully Human Monoclonal Antibody IL-4Ralpha, Atopic Dermatitis Patients Normal Volunteers. CPT Pharmacometrics Syst Pharmacol. 2016;5(11):617-624. doi:10.1002/psp4.12136 Description: Dupilumab exploratory population PK model (Kovalenko 2016; 2-cmt parallel linear + Michaelis-Menten elimination) Article: CPT Pharmacometrics Syst Pharmacol. 2016;5(11):617-624 (open access via PMC5655850)

title: Kovalenko_2016_dupilumab
text: Kovalenko 2016 pooled two Phase 1 studies healthy volunteers four Phase 2 studies patients moderate--severe atopic dermatitis (AD). analysis dataset comprised 197 participants (96 female / 101 male; mean age 37 years; mean weight 76 kg) contributing 2,518 serum dupilumab measurements. Source studies NCT01015027 (R668--0907; single IV 1, 3, 8, 12 mg/kg single SC 150 300 mg), NCT01259323 (R668-AD-0914; 4x weekly SC 75, 150, 300 mg), NCT01385657 (R668-AD-1026; 4x weekly SC 150 300 mg), NCT01484600 (R668-HV-1108; single SC 300 mg), NCT01548404 (R668-AD-1117; 12x weekly SC 300 mg), NCT01639040 (R668-AD-1121; 4x weekly SC 100 300 mg topical corticosteroids). Baseline demographics Results > Data subsection (page 619); per-study dosing sampling designs Table 1. information available programmatically via readModelDb("Kovalenko_2016_dupilumab")$population.

title: Kovalenko_2016_dupilumab
text: Every structural parameter, covariate effect, IIV variance, residual-error term comes Kovalenko 2016 Table 2, column “BLQ data included” (primary model; “BLQ data excluded” column explicitly-labelled sensitivity analysis). column header “omega^2” Table 2 indicates tabulated values variances log-scale random effects, matches nlmixr2 stores right-hand side ~ operator. therefore inserted verbatim (squaring) ini(). “sigma^2 proportional (CV%)” row reports coefficient variation proportional residual, 24.2 % maps SD 0.242 linear scale, propSd stores.

title: Kovalenko_2016_dupilumab
text: Kovalenko 2016 uses rate-based parameterization (ke, k23, k32) rather CL/Q/Vp parameterization canonical nlmixr2lib. model file preserves paper’s parameterization verbatim. Derived quantities agree Table 2’s “Derived parameters” footnote: Linear clearance: CL = ke * V2 = 0.0459 * 2.74 = 0.126 L/day (Table 2: 0.126). Intercompartmental clearance: Q = k23 * V2 = 0.0652 * 2.74 = 0.179 L/day (Table 2: 0.179). Peripheral volume: V3 = V2 * k23 / k32 = 2.74 * 0.0652 / 0.129 = 1.385 L (Table 2: 1.38). MM elimination term central * vmax / (Km + central/vc) mass-rate form consistent vmax reported mg/L/day (see Methods: “unit maximum target-mediated elimination rate (Vm) mg/L/d, can interpreted assumption proportional relationship production rate target volume central compartment”).

title: Kovalenko_2016_dupilumab
text: Three single-dose cohorts simulated, covering one representative regimen Table 1: 3 mg/kg single IV infusion (FIH rich-sampling cohort), 300 mg single SC injection (healthy-volunteer FIH / HV-1108 cohort), labelled-adjacent 300 mg SC weekly x4 (AD-0914 / AD-1026 style regimen). Body weight drawn truncated normal centred paper’s 75 kg reference weight.
code: set.seed(20260424) n_subj <- 100  make_cohort <- function(n, cohort_label, id_offset = 0L) {   tibble::tibble(     id      = id_offset + seq_len(n),     WT      = pmin(pmax(rnorm(n, mean = 76, sd = 15), 45), 130),     cohort  = cohort_label   ) }  cohort_iv   <- make_cohort(n_subj, "3 mg/kg IV single",  id_offset = 0L) cohort_sc   <- make_cohort(n_subj, "300 mg SC single",   id_offset = 100L) cohort_qwx4 <- make_cohort(n_subj, "300 mg SC qw x4",    id_offset = 200L)  # Observation grid - dense early, sparse out to ~85 days (FIH sampling horizon) obs_times <- sort(unique(c(   seq(0, 2, by = 1/24),         # hours 0-48   seq(2.5, 14, by = 0.5),        # early phase   seq(15, 85, by = 1)            # long tail )))  # IV cohort: 3 mg/kg as an instantaneous "bolus" to central at day 0 ev_iv <- cohort_iv |>   dplyr::mutate(time = 0, amt = 3 * WT, cmt = "central", evid = 1L) |>   dplyr::bind_rows(     tidyr::crossing(cohort_iv, time = obs_times) |>       dplyr::mutate(amt = 0, cmt = NA_character_, evid = 0L)   )  # SC single cohort: 300 mg to depot at day 0 ev_sc <- cohort_sc |>   dplyr::mutate(time = 0, amt = 300, cmt = "depot", evid = 1L) |>   dplyr::bind_rows(     tidyr::crossing(cohort_sc, time = obs_times) |>       dplyr::mutate(amt = 0, cmt = NA_character_, evid = 0L)   )  # SC weekly x4 cohort dose_times_qw <- c(0, 7, 14, 21) ev_qwx4 <- cohort_qwx4 |>   tidyr::crossing(time = dose_times_qw) |>   dplyr::mutate(amt = 300, cmt = "depot", evid = 1L) |>   dplyr::bind_rows(     tidyr::crossing(cohort_qwx4, time = obs_times) |>       dplyr::mutate(amt = 0, cmt = NA_character_, evid = 0L)   )  events <- dplyr::bind_rows(ev_iv, ev_sc, ev_qwx4) |>   dplyr::arrange(id, time, dplyr::desc(evid)) |>   dplyr::select(id, time, amt, cmt, evid, WT, cohort)  stopifnot(!anyDuplicated(unique(events[, c("id", "time", "evid")])))

title: Kovalenko_2016_dupilumab
code: mod <- rxode2::rxode2(readModelDb("Kovalenko_2016_dupilumab")) #> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()' sim <- rxode2::rxSolve(mod, events = events, keep = c("WT", "cohort"))

what: Figure replication - concentration-time profiles by regimen
title: Kovalenko_2016_dupilumab
text: Kovalenko 2016 Figure 3 shows examples log-scaled dupilumab concentration- time profiles different single- multiple-dose regimens, spanning target-mediated phase concentrations approach LLOQ (0.078 mg/L). plot reproduces 5-50-95 percentile envelopes three virtual cohorts .  characteristic target-mediated terminal phase (sharp decline ~1 mg/L) reproduces shape reported Kovalenko 2016 Figure 3.
code: vpc <- sim |>   dplyr::filter(!is.na(Cc), time > 0) |>   dplyr::group_by(cohort, time) |>   dplyr::summarise(     Q05 = stats::quantile(Cc, 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),     Q50 = stats::quantile(Cc, 0.50, na.rm = TRUE),     Q95 = stats::quantile(Cc, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   )  ggplot(vpc, aes(time, Q50)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = pmax(Q05, 1e-3), ymax = Q95), alpha = 0.2, fill = "#4682b4") +   geom_line(colour = "#4682b4", linewidth = 0.7) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0.078, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +   facet_wrap(~cohort, scales = "fixed") +   scale_y_log10() +   labs(     x = "Time (days)",     y = "Dupilumab Cc (mg/L, log scale)",     title = "Replicates Figure 3 of Kovalenko 2016 - representative single and repeat-dose profiles",     caption = "Virtual cohorts of N = 100 each. Dashed line = assay LLOQ 0.078 mg/L."   ) +   theme_minimal()
