title: Yamada_2025_zolbetuximab
text: Simulated vs. published GMRs (Q2W relative Q3W, steady-state 42-day interval). simulated GMRs expected track published values within ~10-15%. paper’s Table 2 values come much larger virtual population (1000s subjects) run structural model; cohort size covariate distribution differences explain small residual gap.
code: per_id <- sim_q3w %>%   filter(time >= 105, time <= 147) %>%   group_by(id) %>%   summarise(     Cmax_q3w = max(Cc, na.rm = TRUE),     Cmin_q3w = min(Cc[time > 105.01], na.rm = TRUE),     AUC_q3w  = sum(diff(time) * (head(Cc, -1) + tail(Cc, -1)) / 2, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups  = "drop"   ) %>%   inner_join(     sim_q2w %>%       filter(time >= 112, time <= 154) %>%       group_by(id) %>%       summarise(         Cmax_q2w = max(Cc, na.rm = TRUE),         Cmin_q2w = min(Cc[time > 112.01], na.rm = TRUE),         AUC_q2w  = sum(diff(time) * (head(Cc, -1) + tail(Cc, -1)) / 2, na.rm = TRUE),         .groups  = "drop"       ),     by = "id"   )  gmr <- function(num, den) exp(mean(log(num) - log(den)))  comparison <- tibble(   Parameter     = c("Cmax", "Cmin (Ctrough)", "AUC42d"),   `GMR (sim)`   = c(gmr(per_id$Cmax_q2w, per_id$Cmax_q3w),                     gmr(per_id$Cmin_q2w, per_id$Cmin_q3w),                     gmr(per_id$AUC_q2w,  per_id$AUC_q3w)),   `GMR (Yamada 2025 Table 2)` = c(0.792, 1.192, 1.000) )  knitr::kable(comparison, digits = 3,              caption = "Simulated vs. published GMRs (Q2W relative to Q3W, steady-state 42-day interval).")

previous_headings: Zolbetuximab population PK in gastric / GEJ adenocarcinoma
title: Yamada_2025_zolbetuximab
text: Yamada 2025 publish individual PK per-subject covariate values, virtual population approximates paper’s reference population rather reproducing : BSA: normal around 1.70 m^2 (Figure 1 reference), SD 0.18, clipped 1.20-2.40 m^2. paper report BSA calculation method (DuBois / Mosteller / Haycock); assume distribution insensitive choice. ALB, HGB, TBILI sampled symmetric distributions centered Figure 1 reference values (39.1 g/L, 118 g/L, 0.38 mg/dL). TBILI drawn log-normal match positive-skewed distribution expected oncology. SEXF ~ Bernoulli(0.34), matching ~33-35% female fraction reported across SPOTLIGHT GLOW. PRIOR_GAST ~ Bernoulli(0.30). paper reports ~30% pooled PK population prior gastrectomy. COMB_EOX ~ Bernoulli(0.04). EOX backbone uncommon phase 3 studies; mFOLFOX6 CAPOX dominate. Residual error interpretation: Table 1 reports “Proportional error 0.169” “Additive error 4.03 ug/mL”. Following Thakre_2022_risankizumab convention already established repo, proportional value interpreted NONMEM $SIGMA variance (propSd stored ini() sqrt(0.169)), additive value interpreted SD ug/mL (consistent column-header unit [ug/mL]). standard nlmixr2lib convention; alternative interpretation numbers SDs directly reduce proportional SD factor ~0.411. Time units: paper reports clearances L/h Kdecay 1/day. use time = "day" internally align Kdecay, multiply clearance estimates 24 convert L/h → L/day. algebraic change ; derived half-life exposure metrics invariant. Dummy-variable covariate effects encoded 1 + theta·DUMMY (linear fractional change), matching NONMEM additive dummy-variable form Table 1 “gastrectomy” / “female” / “EOX” phrasing implies. alternative interpretation ((1 + theta)^DUMMY) indistinguishable binary covariates gives identical predictions model.

title: Yamada_2025_zolbetuximab
text: Structure: two-compartment zero-order IV input. Time-dependent total clearance: CL(t) = CLss + CLT·exp(-Kdecay·t), declines ~0.028 L/h baseline (roughly CLss + CLT) ~0.012 L/h steady state tumor-associated target-mediated clearance saturates first several cycles. Reference subject terminal half-life: ~7.6 days baseline (ADC-driven early CL) extending ~15.2 days steady state time-dependent component decays, consistent 7.56-15.2 day range paper reports. Strongest covariates: BSA clearances volumes (exponents 1.06 / 0.968), ALB CLss (-0.535) Kdecay (1.48), HGB V1 (-0.374), COMB V1 (+0.466 EOX backbone). Immunogenicity, race, mild/moderate renal impairment, mild hepatic impairment evaluated retained covariates.

text: Yamada , Takeuchi M, Komatsu K, Bonate PL, Poondru S, Yang J. Population PK Exposure-Response Analyses Zolbetuximab Patients Locally Advanced Unresectable Metastatic G/GEJ Adenocarcinoma. Clinical Translational Science. 2025;18(7):e70280. doi:10.1111/cts.70280

title: Yang_2021_cemiplimab
text: Citation: Yang F, Paccaly AJ, Rippley RK, Davis JD, DiCioccio . Population pharmacokinetic characteristics cemiplimab patients advanced malignancies. J Pharmacokinet Pharmacodyn. 2021;48(4):479-494. doi:[10.1007/s10928-021-09739-y](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10928-021-09739-y) Description: Two-compartment population PK model cemiplimab (anti-PD-1 IgG4) time-varying clearance (sigmoid Emax) adults advanced solid tumors including cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (CSCC). Modality: Therapeutic monoclonal antibody (IgG4), 30-min IV infusion. Cemiplimab fully human anti-PD-1 IgG4 monoclonal antibody. Yang 2021 develops final population PK model used support fixed-dose 350 mg Q3W selection, pooling 11,178 PK observations 548 patients across two studies (1423 1540). Structure: linear two-compartment IV model time-varying CL via sigmoid-Emax function time since first dose: CLt,=CLbase,⋅exp⁡(Emax⁡,itγT50,iγ+tγ),Emax⁡,=Emax⁡,REF⋅exp⁡(ηEmax,),T50,=T50,REF⋅exp⁡(ηT50,) \mathrm{CL}_{t,} \;=\; \mathrm{CL}_{\mathrm{base},} \cdot   \exp\!\left( \dfrac{E_{\max,}\, t^{\gamma}}                      {T_{50,}^{\gamma} + t^{\gamma}} \right), \qquad E_{\max,} = E_{\max,\mathrm{REF}} \cdot \exp(\eta_{E_{\max},}), \qquad T_{50,}   = T_{50,\mathrm{REF}}   \cdot \exp(\eta_{T_{50},}) Emax=−0.410E_{\max} = -0.410 (log-fold maximal decrease, asymptote exp(EmaxE_{\max}) ≈0.66\approx 0.66 baseline CL) T50=28.9T_{50} = 28.9 days. Covariate effects (power-form) shared CL/Q: baseline body weight, albumin, IgG, ALT. shared V2/V3: baseline body weight, BMI.

title: Yang_2021_cemiplimab
text: final-model population comprised 548 patients across 2 trials (Yang 2021 Table 1): Phase 1 first--human Study 1423 (NCT02383212; 396 patients advanced malignancies; weight-based fixed dosing arms). Phase 2 Study 1540 (NCT02760498; 152 patients advanced cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma). Dose range: 1, 3, 10 mg/kg Q2W; 3 mg/kg Q3W; 200 mg Q2W; 350 mg Q3W (30-min IV infusions). Baseline demographics (Yang 2021 Table 2): Median age 65 (range 27-96) years. 60.4% male, 39.6% female. Race: 90.9% White, 3.6% Black, 1.6% Asian, 3.8% . Median weight 76.2 (range 30.9-172) kg. Median BMI 26.5 (range 14.8-56.3) kg/m^2. Median albumin 38.0 (range 22.0-48.0) g/L. Median IgG 9.65 (range 1.29-27.9) g/L. Median ALT 20.0 (range 5.00-196) IU/L. patients (80.3%) received cemiplimab 3 mg/kg Q2W. metadata available programmatically via readModelDb("Yang_2021_cemiplimab")$population.

title: Yang_2021_cemiplimab
text: Per-parameter origins recorded -file comments next ini() entry inst/modeldb/specificDrugs/Yang_2021_cemiplimab.R. table collects one place review. Equations: structural two-compartment micro-constant form. final-model equations CL_i, Q_i, V2_i, V3_i, T50_i, Emax_i appear “Final PopPK model” section Yang 2021. Reference covariate values (denominators power terms) population medians Yang 2021 Table 2: WT 76.2 kg, ALB 38 g/L, IGG 9.65 g/L, ALT 20 IU/L, BMI 26.5 kg/m^2.

title: Yang_2021_cemiplimab
text: Original observed data publicly available. simulations use virtual cohort whose demographics approximate Yang 2021 population (Table 2). Continuous covariates drawn log-normal distributions anchored reported median plausible scale; distributions clipped reported ranges. Two reference dosing regimens compared: 3 mg/kg Q2W (weight-based regimen used 80.3% analysis population) 350 mg Q3W (fixed regimen ultimately recommended).
code: set.seed(2021) n_subj <- 250  cohort <- tibble(   ID  = seq_len(n_subj),   WT  = pmin(pmax(rlnorm(n_subj, log(76.2), 0.27), 30.9), 172),   BMI = pmin(pmax(rlnorm(n_subj, log(26.5), 0.22), 14.8), 56.3),   ALB = pmin(pmax(rnorm(n_subj, 38.0, 5.0),         22.0), 48.0),   IGG = pmin(pmax(rlnorm(n_subj, log(9.65), 0.30),  1.29), 27.9),   ALT = pmin(pmax(rlnorm(n_subj, log(20.0), 0.55),  5.00), 196) ) build_events <- function(pop, dose_mode = c("3mgkg_q2w", "350mg_q3w")) {   dose_mode <- match.arg(dose_mode)   if (dose_mode == "3mgkg_q2w") {     interval_d <- 14     n_doses    <- 12     amt        <- pop$WT * 3     label      <- "3 mg/kg Q2W"   } else {     interval_d <- 21     n_doses    <- 8     amt        <- rep(350, nrow(pop))     label      <- "350 mg Q3W"   }   dose_times <- seq(0, by = interval_d, length.out = n_doses)   obs_times  <- sort(unique(c(dose_times, seq(0, interval_d * n_doses, by = 1))))   d_dose <- pop |>     mutate(AMT = amt) |>     tidyr::crossing(TIME = dose_times) |>     mutate(EVID = 1, CMT = "central", DUR = 0.5 / 24, DV = NA_real_,            treatment = label)   d_obs <- pop |>     tidyr::crossing(TIME = obs_times) |>     mutate(AMT = NA_real_, EVID = 0, CMT = "central", DUR = NA_real_,            DV = NA_real_, treatment = label)   dplyr::bind_rows(d_dose, d_obs) |>     dplyr::arrange(ID, TIME, dplyr::desc(EVID)) |>     as.data.frame() }  events_q2w <- build_events(cohort, "3mgkg_q2w") events_q3w <- build_events(cohort, "350mg_q3w")

title: Yang_2021_cemiplimab
code: mod <- readModelDb("Yang_2021_cemiplimab") sim_q2w <- rxSolve(mod, events = events_q2w, returnType = "data.frame") #> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()' sim_q3w <- rxSolve(mod, events = events_q3w, returnType = "data.frame") #> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()' sim <- dplyr::bind_rows(   dplyr::mutate(sim_q2w, treatment = "3 mg/kg Q2W"),   dplyr::mutate(sim_q3w, treatment = "350 mg Q3W") )

text: Yang 2021 Figure 5 shows visual predictive checks dose group Figure 7 overlays observed-vs-simulated profiles 350 mg Q3W. figure reproduces median 5-95% prediction interval packaged model two regimens informed fixed-dose decision.
code: sim_summary <- sim |>   dplyr::filter(time > 0) |>   dplyr::group_by(time, treatment) |>   dplyr::summarise(     median = stats::median(Cc, na.rm = TRUE),     lo     = stats::quantile(Cc, 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),     hi     = stats::quantile(Cc, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   )  ggplot(sim_summary, aes(time, median, colour = treatment, fill = treatment)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lo, ymax = hi), alpha = 0.15, colour = NA) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1) +   labs(     x = "Time since first dose (days)",     y = "Cemiplimab concentration (mg/L)",     title = "Simulated cemiplimab PK: 3 mg/kg Q2W vs 350 mg Q3W",     subtitle = paste0("Median and 90% prediction interval (N = ", n_subj,                       " virtual patients per arm). Compare with Yang 2021 Figs. 5 and 7."),     caption = "Model: Yang 2021 J Pharmacokinet Pharmacodyn 48(4):479-494"   ) +   theme_bw()

title: Yang_2021_cemiplimab
text: Yang 2021 reports cemiplimab CL decreased average 35.9% time relative baseline (0.326 0.208 L/day within 16 weeks treatment). Emax=−0.410E_{\max} = -0.410, asymptotic CL t≫T50t \gg T_{50} CLbase⋅exp⁡(−0.410)=0.664\mathrm{CL}_{\mathrm{base}} \cdot \exp(-0.410) = 0.664 baseline (33.6% reduction). typical-value CL(t) profile reproduces time course typical reference subject (median covariates, deterministic, etas = 0).
code: t_grid <- seq(0, 365, by = 5) events_cl <- data.frame(   ID   = 1,   WT   = 76.2,   BMI  = 26.5,   ALB  = 38,   IGG  = 9.65,   ALT  = 20,   TIME = c(0, t_grid),   AMT  = c(350, rep(NA_real_, length(t_grid))),   EVID = c(1, rep(0, length(t_grid))),   CMT  = "central",   DUR  = c(0.5 / 24, rep(NA_real_, length(t_grid))),   DV   = NA_real_ ) mod_typ <- rxode2::zeroRe(mod) #> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()' sim_cl  <- rxSolve(mod_typ, events = events_cl, returnType = "data.frame") #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalcl', 'etalvc', 'etaemax', 'etalt50' sim_cl  <- sim_cl[sim_cl$time > 0, ]  ggplot(sim_cl, aes(time, cl / cl_base)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1, colour = "steelblue") +   geom_hline(yintercept = 1,           linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +   geom_hline(yintercept = exp(-0.410), linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +   geom_vline(xintercept = 16 * 7,      linetype = "dotted", colour = "darkred") +   labs(     x = "Time since first dose (days)",     y = "CL(t) / CL(0)",     title = "Time-varying clearance in Yang 2021",     subtitle = paste0("Typical reference subject. Asymptote at exp(Emax) = ",                       round(exp(-0.410), 3),                       " (33.6% reduction). Red dotted line: 16-week mark.")   ) +   theme_bw()

title: Yang_2021_cemiplimab
text: Yang 2021 Table 4 reports post-hoc steady-state AUC 6 weeks trough concentrations body-weight quartile 3 mg/kg Q2W 350 mg Q3W. packaged model used compute simulation-based NCA 6-week steady-state window dose regimens, results compared published values next section. Simulated NCA steady state (6-week window), 3 mg/kg Q2W Simulated NCA steady state (6-week window), 350 mg Q3W
code: nca_window <- function(sim, dose_interval_d, n_doses, label) {   ss_start <- dose_interval_d * (n_doses - 3)            # last 3 doses span 6 weeks for both regimens   ss_end   <- ss_start + 42                              # 6 weeks = 42 days   sim_ss <- sim |>     dplyr::filter(treatment == label,                   !is.na(Cc),                   time >= ss_start,                   time <= ss_end) |>     dplyr::mutate(time_rel = time - ss_start) |>     dplyr::select(id, time_rel, Cc) |>     dplyr::distinct(id, time_rel, .keep_all = TRUE)   conc_obj <- PKNCA::PKNCAconc(sim_ss, Cc ~ time_rel | id)   cohort_with_dose <- cohort |>     dplyr::transmute(id = ID,                      amt = if (label == "3 mg/kg Q2W") WT * 3 else 350)   dose_df <- cohort_with_dose |>     tidyr::crossing(time_rel = seq(0, by = dose_interval_d, length.out = ceiling(42 / dose_interval_d)))   dose_obj <- PKNCA::PKNCAdose(as.data.frame(dose_df), amt ~ time_rel | id)   intervals <- data.frame(start = 0, end = 42,                           cmax = TRUE, cmin = TRUE,                           tmax = TRUE, auclast = TRUE,                           half.life = TRUE)   nca_data <- PKNCA::PKNCAdata(conc_obj, dose_obj, intervals = intervals)   PKNCA::pk.nca(nca_data) }  nca_q2w <- nca_window(sim, 14, 12, "3 mg/kg Q2W") #>  ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■               59% |  ETA:  2s nca_q3w <- nca_window(sim, 21,  8, "350 mg Q3W") #>  ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■             68% |  ETA:  2s  knitr::kable(summary(nca_q2w),              caption = "Simulated NCA at steady state (6-week window), 3 mg/kg Q2W") knitr::kable(summary(nca_q3w),              caption = "Simulated NCA at steady state (6-week window), 350 mg Q3W")

title: Yang_2021_cemiplimab
text: Yang 2021 Table 4 reports following post-hoc estimates AUC6wk,ss\mathrm{AUC}_{6\mathrm{wk,ss}} Ctrough,ssC_{\mathrm{trough,ss}} body-weight quartile, pooled across weight strata (overall N=548): Computed roughly mean four quartile means Yang 2021 Table 4 (Q1-Q4). packaged model’s PKNCA AUC_last Cmin / predose values 6-week window expected range (within ~20%); residual discrepancies reflect () difference AUC_6wk,ss paper computes (post-hoc individual exposure averaged steady-state regimen) versus PKNCA AUC_last single 6-week interval starting first dose interval, (b) virtual-cohort covariate distribution exactly reproducing joint distribution WT, ALB, IGG, ALT, BMI Yang 2021 patients. two-regimen exposure equivalence (similar mean AUC_6wk,ss C_trough,ss 3 mg/kg Q2W 350 mg Q3W) central finding informing fixed-dose 350 mg Q3W selection. regimens produce overlapping prediction-interval bands concentration-time plot virtual cohort whose median weight equals population median (76.2 kg).

title: Yang_2021_cemiplimab
text: BLK_ON_T50 race effect T50 implemented. Yang 2021’s final-model equation T50,iT_{50,} written T50,REF⋅(BLK)BLK_ON_T50⋅exp⁡(η)T_{50,\mathrm{REF}} \cdot (\mathrm{BLK})^{\mathrm{BLK\_ON\_T50}} \cdot \exp(\eta) BLK_ON_T50 = 1.01 (RSE 29.2%). formula internally inconsistent: BLK 0/1 indicator (Black = 1, non-Black = 0), 0^1.01 = 0 zero T50 96.4% non-Black patients cohort. paper show NONMEM control stream supplementary DOCX available disk, intended encoding verified. paper states retained covariates “limited impact (< 20%) cemiplimab exposure” “clinically meaningful” given flat exposure-response relationship. packaged model therefore uses T50,=T50,REF⋅exp⁡(ηT50)T_{50,} = T_{50,\mathrm{REF}} \cdot \exp(\eta_{T_{50}}) races. Datasets RACE_BLACK column ignored model; immaterial omission per paper’s assessment clinical impact. Time-varying covariates kept baseline. Yang 2021 mentions body weight, albumin, lactate dehydrogenase assessed time-varying covariates addition baseline values. final-model equations expose baseline values (WGTBL, ALBBL, IGGBL, ALTBL, BMIBL), packaged model treats time-fixed. Setting WT, ALB, etc. time-varying user data treat supplied values literally. Reference covariate values. Yang 2021’s final-model equations written generically (WGTBL_i / WGTBL_REF)^..., etc. paper state reference values numerically equations, simulation section uses median values Table 2 (WT 76.2 kg, ALB 38 g/L, IGG 9.65 g/L, ALT 20 IU/L, BMI 26.5 kg/m^2). packaged model uses medians reference. (paper’s separate simulation section reports slightly different mean weight 75 kg used fixed-dose simulations; difference immaterial exposures reported quartile.) Residual error parameterization. Yang 2021 reports residual variability Y = F + F * ERR(1) + ERR(2) “log-transformation error model”. packaged model implements Cc ~ add(addSd) + prop(propSd), standard combined additive-plus-proportional residual error nlmixr2’s linear-space formulation. VPC-style simulation difference log-transformed linear-space combined-error forms immaterial; likelihood-based estimation new data, users require strict log-transform residual-error model adapt model accordingly. IV infusion duration. Yang 2021 administered cemiplimab 30-min IV infusions across dose groups. vignette simulations use DUR = 0.5 / 24 day match. Virtual-cohort marginals. Continuous covariates (WT, BMI, ALB, IGG, ALT) simulated independent log-normal / normal distributions anchored reported population medians clipped published ranges. joint covariate structure (e.g., correlation WT BMI) preserved.

title: Axatilimab semimechanistic PK/PD model (Yang 2024)
text: Citation: Yang Y, Sokolov V, Volkova , et al. Semimechanistic Population PK/PD Modeling Axatilimab Healthy Participants Patients Solid Tumors Chronic Graft-Versus-Host Disease. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 2025;117(3):704-714. doi:10.1002/cpt.3503 Article: https://doi.org/10.1002/cpt.3503 — Yang et al., Clin Pharmacol Ther 117(3):704-714, 2025 (PubMed PMID 39704205). Supplemental MLXTRAN code: open-access supplement bundled article. model semimechanistic population PK/PD model integrates axatilimab pharmacokinetics dynamics four pharmacodynamic biomarkers: CSF-1 — cytokine ligand CSF-1R; rises sharply axatilimab dosing receptor blockade reduces CSF-1 clearance. NCMC — nonclassical CD14+/CD16++ monocytic cell count; efficacy biomarker, decreased axatilimab via CSF-1R signaling blockade. AST CPK — safety / liver--muscle enzyme biomarkers, predicted rise Kupffer-cell macrophages depleted axatilimab longer clear circulating enzymes. PK structure two-compartment IV disposition model parallel linear CSF-1R-mediated saturable elimination. Saturable clearance parameterized Hill-type fractional occupancy axatilimab CSF-1 compete binding CSF-1R (Cc_nM/Kd_PK raised Hill coefficient Nh = 2.5).

title: Axatilimab semimechanistic PK/PD model (Yang 2024)
text: pooled analysis dataset 325 participants across four phase 1 / 1-2 / 2 trials: Demographics Yang 2024 Table S3 (continuous) Table S4 (categorical): Age: median 55 years (range 7-81); 7 cGVHD subjects children aged 7-15. Body weight: median 73.6 kg (range 18.1-151). Sex: 39.4% female (n=128 / 325). Race: 82.5% White, 5.5% Asian, 4.3% Black, 1.8% , 5.8% Unknown. Baseline CSF-1: median 549 pg/mL (range 39-4690 pg/mL). Baseline NCMC: median 19 cells/μL (range 0-202). Baseline AST: median 28 U/L (range 11-164). Baseline CPK: median 63 U/L (range 13-1069). ADA (ever-positive): 40% overall (50% / 36% / 40% healthy / cancer / cGVHD). Doses ranged 0.15 6 mg/kg IV, single-dose every 2 / 4 weeks. approved cGVHD dose 0.3 mg/kg Q2W. information available programmatically via model’s population metadata (readModelDb("Yang_2024_axatilimab")()$meta$population model loaded).
code: mod_meta <- readModelDb("Yang_2024_axatilimab")()$meta str(mod_meta$population, max.level = 1) #> List of 14 #>  $ n_subjects                  : int 325 #>  $ n_studies                   : int 4 #>  $ age_range                   : chr "7-81 years (7 children aged 7-15 with cGVHD)" #>  $ age_median                  : chr "55 years" #>  $ weight_range                : chr "18.1-151 kg" #>  $ weight_median               : chr "73.6 kg" #>  $ sex_female_pct              : num 39.4 #>  $ race_ethnicity              : Named num [1:5] 82.5 5.5 4.3 1.8 5.8 #>   ..- attr(*, "names")= chr [1:5] "White" "Asian" "Black" "Other" ... #>  $ disease_state               : chr "Pooled cohort: 14 healthy adults (SNDX-6352-0001), 33 adults with advanced or metastatic solid tumors (SNDX-635"| __truncated__ #>  $ dose_range                  : chr "0.15 to 6 mg/kg IV; single dose, every 2 weeks (Q2W), or every 4 weeks (Q4W); 0.3 mg/kg Q2W is the approved cGVHD regimen." #>  $ regions                     : chr "Multi-regional (multi-center US-led trials; phase 1 healthy-volunteer study had EudraCT registration in Europe)." #>  $ n_observations              :List of 5 #>  $ n_observations_below_LOQ_pct: num 35.5 #>  $ notes                       : chr "Baseline demographics from Yang 2024 Tables S3 and S4. ADA prevalence (ever-positive) 40% overall. Plasma axati"| __truncated__

title: Axatilimab semimechanistic PK/PD model (Yang 2024)
text: Every ini() value inst/modeldb/specificDrugs/Yang_2024_axatilimab.R cites Yang 2024 Table 1 row trailing comment. table collects equations parameter origins review:

title: Axatilimab semimechanistic PK/PD model (Yang 2024)
text: following internal inconsistencies detected extraction. model file uses value consistent final-model parameter table supplemental MLXTRAN code; discrepancies recorded traceability. kada value contradiction (page 708 narrative vs Table 1, MLXTRAN, formal covariate-effect summary). Page 708 published article states “ADA effect coefficient (kada) 2.1 (relative standard error (RSE), 2.92%), indicating axatilimab clearance increased approximately 3.1-fold ADA status positive.” However, Table 1 lists kada = 0.489 (RSE 3.52%, 95% CI 0.455-0.522), formal covariate-effect summary page 710 reports “50.6% (95% CI, 47.0-54.2) increase CL” positive ADA status, supplemental MLXTRAN code (Supplement 2) implements CL * (1 + k_ada * ADACN) k_ada = 0.489. factor (1 + 0.489) gives 48.9% CL increase, matching 50.6% covariate-effect summary (small difference additional contribution baseline CSF-1 covariance). model file uses kada = 0.489 canonical value; page-708 “2.1 / 3.1-fold” statement treated typographical error. Figure 3 CSF-1 covariate axis units. Figure 3 article labels CSF-1 covariate axis “ng/mL” displayed 90th-percentile = 1060 10th-percentile = 325. Table S3 reports baseline CSF-1 ng/L (= pg/mL) overall median 549 (range 39-4690). Figure 3 numerical values match pg/mL (=ng/L), ng/mL — typical plasma CSF-1 / M-CSF concentrations 100-2000 pg/mL, 1060 ng/mL three orders magnitude physiologic. model file’s covariateData[[CSF1]]$units pg/mL reference value 549 pg/mL (matching Table S3, dimensionally consistent interpretation). Unit reformulation Vmax amount-rate term. published Eq. 1 uses Ac (axatilimab amount μg) explicit Vd × MW_PK / convF_PK factor convF_PK = 0.01/1.5 (supplement’s MW_PK = 150 kDa representation). model file works mg central peripheral1 amounts (Cc = central / vc directly mg/L = μg/mL), saturable-clearance amount-rate term rewritten Vmax × C1 × vc × MW_PK / 1000 (mg/h). mathematically equivalent Yang 2024 Eq. 1 — Vmax × C1 × vc (nmol/L/h × L) = nmol/h, MW_PK / 1000 (= 0.150 mg/nmol) converts nmol/h mg/h.

title: Axatilimab semimechanistic PK/PD model (Yang 2024)
text: original individual-level data publicly available; figures use small typical-value virtual cohorts whose covariate values match population medians paper-specific reference values.
code: make_typical <- function(dose_mg_kg, regimen, weight_kg = 75,                          dis_cancer = 0L, dis_hv = 0L, ada_pos = 0L,                          CSF1_pgmL = 549, CPK_UL = 63,                          duration_days = 28, sample_h = 4) {   # Mass dose: dose_mg_kg * body weight (mg)   amt_mg <- dose_mg_kg * weight_kg   ii_h   <- if (regimen == "Q2W") 24 * 14 else 24 * 28   end_h  <- duration_days * 24    # Build event table: dose at t=0 (and addl as appropriate), Cc-typed observations   # over the simulation window so rxSolve can resolve the multi-DVID model.   n_doses <- floor(end_h / ii_h) + 1   events <- rxode2::et()   for (i in seq_len(n_doses)) {     events <- rxode2::et(events, amt = amt_mg, cmt = "central", evid = 1,                          time = (i - 1) * ii_h)   }   events <- rxode2::et(events, seq(0, end_h, by = sample_h), cmt = "Cc")   events$WT         <- weight_kg   events$CSF1       <- CSF1_pgmL   events$CPK        <- CPK_UL   events$DIS_CANCER <- dis_cancer   events$DIS_HV     <- dis_hv   events$ADA_POS    <- ada_pos   events$regimen    <- paste0(dose_mg_kg, " mg/kg ", regimen)   events }

title: Axatilimab semimechanistic PK/PD model (Yang 2024)
text: model loaded used typical-value (-subject variability) form figure replications.
code: mod <- readModelDb("Yang_2024_axatilimab")() |> rxode2::zeroRe()

what: Replicate Figure 4 — axatilimab + biomarker time courses by dose
title: Axatilimab semimechanistic PK/PD model (Yang 2024)
text: Yang 2024 Figure 4 shows steady-state time course single 28-day dosing interval axatilimab Cc, NCMC, CSF-1 four regimens: 0.15 / 0.3 / 1 mg/kg Q2W 3 mg/kg Q4W. simulation carries six dose intervals system approached steady state, reports final 28-day window.
code: sim_one <- function(events) {   s <- rxode2::rxSolve(mod, events = events, returnType = "data.frame", keep = "regimen")   # Drop dose-row duplicates that have no Cc value   s |> dplyr::filter(!is.na(Cc)) }  # Pre-saturation: 12 weeks of repeated dosing followed by a 28-day window fig4_events <- dplyr::bind_rows(   sim_one(make_typical(0.15, "Q2W", duration_days = 12 * 7))      |> dplyr::mutate(regimen = "0.15 mg/kg Q2W"),   sim_one(make_typical(0.30, "Q2W", duration_days = 12 * 7))      |> dplyr::mutate(regimen = "0.3 mg/kg Q2W"),   sim_one(make_typical(1.00, "Q2W", duration_days = 12 * 7))      |> dplyr::mutate(regimen = "1 mg/kg Q2W"),   sim_one(make_typical(3.00, "Q4W", duration_days = 12 * 7))      |> dplyr::mutate(regimen = "3 mg/kg Q4W") ) #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk' #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk' #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk' #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk'  # Restrict to the final 28-day window and re-zero time fig4 <- fig4_events |>   dplyr::group_by(regimen) |>   dplyr::mutate(t_max = max(time)) |>   dplyr::filter(time >= t_max - 28 * 24) |>   dplyr::mutate(time_d = (time - (t_max - 28 * 24)) / 24) |>   dplyr::ungroup() |>   dplyr::mutate(     csf1_ngmL = csf1obs,    # observation alias already in ng/mL     regimen   = factor(regimen, levels = c("0.15 mg/kg Q2W", "0.3 mg/kg Q2W",                                             "1 mg/kg Q2W",   "3 mg/kg Q4W"))   ) ggplot(fig4, aes(time_d, Cc)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_y_log10() +   facet_wrap(~ regimen, nrow = 1) +   labs(x = "Time within steady-state cycle (day)",        y = "Axatilimab plasma concentration (mg/L = ug/mL)",        title  = "Figure 4A replication — typical-patient axatilimab PK at steady state",        caption = "Replicates Figure 4(a) of Yang 2024.") ggplot(fig4, aes(time_d, ncmc)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8, colour = "darkred") +   facet_wrap(~ regimen, nrow = 1) +   labs(x = "Time within steady-state cycle (day)",        y = "NCMC (cells/uL)",        title  = "Figure 4B replication — typical-patient NCMC at steady state",        caption = "Replicates Figure 4(b) of Yang 2024. Baseline NCMC = 16 cells/uL (cGVHD reference).") ggplot(fig4, aes(time_d, csf1_ngmL)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8, colour = "steelblue") +   facet_wrap(~ regimen, nrow = 1) +   labs(x = "Time within steady-state cycle (day)",        y = "Plasma CSF-1 (ng/mL)",        title  = "Figure 4C replication — typical-patient CSF-1 at steady state",        caption = "Replicates Figure 4(c) of Yang 2024.")

title: Axatilimab semimechanistic PK/PD model (Yang 2024)
text: Yang 2024 Table 1 reports baseline NCMC 16 / 29.7 / 54.2 cells/μL cGVHD / healthy / cancer reference patients. numbers typical-value baseline NCMC (ncmc state t=0 drug) three population indicators: Baseline NCMC population type (typical patient median CSF-1 CPK). ADA-positive CL effect (paper covariate summary: +50.6% CL ADA = 1): Linear CL ADA status. Note: model file’s cl variable already carries ADA factor.
code: baseline_ncmc <- function(dis_cancer, dis_hv) {   ev <- rxode2::et(seq(0, 168, by = 24), cmt = "Cc")   ev$WT <- 75; ev$CSF1 <- 549; ev$CPK <- 63   ev$DIS_CANCER <- dis_cancer; ev$DIS_HV <- dis_hv; ev$ADA_POS <- 0   s <- rxode2::rxSolve(mod, ev, returnType = "data.frame")   s$ncmc[1] }  tibble::tibble(   Population = c("cGVHD reference", "Healthy volunteer", "Advanced solid tumor"),   `BL_NCMC simulated (cells/uL)` = c(     baseline_ncmc(0L, 0L),     baseline_ncmc(0L, 1L),     baseline_ncmc(1L, 0L)   ),   `Yang 2024 Table 1` = c(16, 29.7, 54.2) ) |> knitr::kable(digits = 2,                   caption = "Baseline NCMC by population type (typical patient at median CSF-1 and CPK).") #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk' #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk' #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk' typical_cl <- function(ada) {   # Pull individual CL via a unit-volume dose (1 mg) and reading IPRED at first sample   ev <- rxode2::et(amt = 1, cmt = "central", evid = 1, time = 0) |>     rxode2::et(c(0, 0.001), cmt = "Cc")   ev$WT <- 75; ev$CSF1 <- 549; ev$CPK <- 63   ev$DIS_CANCER <- 0; ev$DIS_HV <- 0; ev$ADA_POS <- ada   s <- rxode2::rxSolve(mod, ev, returnType = "data.frame")   unique(s$cl)[1] } cl_neg <- typical_cl(0L) #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk' cl_pos <- typical_cl(1L) #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk' tibble::tibble(   ADA = c("Negative (ref)", "Positive"),   `Typical CL (L/h)` = c(cl_neg, cl_pos),   `Fractional change vs ADA-` = c(0, (cl_pos - cl_neg) / cl_neg) ) |> knitr::kable(digits = 4,                   caption = "Linear CL by ADA status. Note: the model file's `cl` variable already carries the ADA factor.")

title: Axatilimab semimechanistic PK/PD model (Yang 2024)
text: axatilimab dose, four biomarker states must hold individual baseline values indefinitely (numerical tolerance) — standard endogenous-system invariant. Biomarker steady-state hold (axatilimab dose, 4000 h simulation).
code: ss_ev <- rxode2::et(seq(0, 4000, by = 48), cmt = "Cc") ss_ev$WT <- 75; ss_ev$CSF1 <- 549; ss_ev$CPK <- 63 ss_ev$DIS_CANCER <- 0; ss_ev$DIS_HV <- 0; ss_ev$ADA_POS <- 0 ss <- rxode2::rxSolve(mod, ss_ev, returnType = "data.frame") #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk'  ss_summary <- tibble::tibble(   Biomarker = c("csf1 (nM)", "ncmc (cells/uL)", "ast (U/L)", "cpk (U/L)"),   Min       = c(min(ss$csf1), min(ss$ncmc), min(ss$ast), min(ss$cpk)),   Max       = c(max(ss$csf1), max(ss$ncmc), max(ss$ast), max(ss$cpk)),   Target    = c(0.01, 16, 35.5, 101) ) knitr::kable(ss_summary, digits = 6,              caption = "Biomarker steady-state hold (no axatilimab dose, 4000 h simulation).")

what: Replicate Figure 5 — steady-state NCMC dose response (Q2W)
title: Axatilimab semimechanistic PK/PD model (Yang 2024)
text: Figure 5 Yang 2024 shows steady-state mean trough NCMC concentration vs dose Q2W regimen. simulation sweeps 0.15-3 mg/kg Q2W typical cGVHD patient computes Cavg / Cmin within final dosing interval 16 weeks dosing (eight Q2W cycles sufficient convergence given NCMC turnover halflife log(2) / 0.021 ≈ 33 h).
code: dose_sweep <- c(0.15, 0.3, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.5, 2, 3)  ncmc_metrics <- function(dose_mg_kg) {   ev_long <- make_typical(dose_mg_kg, regimen = "Q2W", duration_days = 16 * 7)   s <- rxode2::rxSolve(mod, ev_long, returnType = "data.frame") |>     dplyr::filter(!is.na(Cc))   end_h    <- max(s$time)   s_final  <- s |> dplyr::filter(time >= end_h - 14 * 24)   tibble::tibble(     dose = dose_mg_kg,     cavg = mean(s_final$ncmc),     cmin = min(s_final$ncmc)   ) }  fig5 <- purrr::map_dfr(dose_sweep, ncmc_metrics) |>   tidyr::pivot_longer(c(cavg, cmin), names_to = "metric", values_to = "ncmc") |>   dplyr::mutate(metric = recode(metric, cavg = "Cavg", cmin = "Cmin")) #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk' #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk' #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk' #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk' #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk' #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk' #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk' #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk'  ggplot(fig5, aes(dose, ncmc, colour = metric)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_point(size = 2) +   labs(x = "Axatilimab dose (mg/kg Q2W)",        y = "Steady-state NCMC (cells/uL)",        colour = NULL,        title  = "Figure 5 replication — steady-state NCMC dose response (typical cGVHD patient)",        caption = "Replicates Figure 5 of Yang 2024 (typical-value, single subject).")

title: Axatilimab semimechanistic PK/PD model (Yang 2024)
text: article report published NCA table axatilimab, PKNCA block functions functional check simulated profile produces dimensionally sensible AUC / Cmax 0.3 mg/kg single-dose regimen. Simulated single-dose 0.3 mg/kg axatilimab NCA, typical cGVHD patient.
code: single_ev <- rxode2::et(amt = 22.5, cmt = "central", evid = 1, time = 0) |>   rxode2::et(c(0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 24, 48, 72, 96, 168, 240, 336),              cmt = "Cc") single_ev$id         <- 1L single_ev$WT         <- 75 single_ev$CSF1       <- 549 single_ev$CPK        <- 63 single_ev$DIS_CANCER <- 0 single_ev$DIS_HV     <- 0 single_ev$ADA_POS    <- 0 single_ev$regimen    <- "0.3 mg/kg single dose" sim_sd <- rxode2::rxSolve(mod, single_ev, returnType = "data.frame", keep = "regimen") |>   dplyr::filter(!is.na(Cc)) |>   dplyr::mutate(id = 1L) #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvc', 'etalcl', 'etalvmax', 'etalbl_csf1', 'etalbl_ncmc', 'etalbl_ast', 'etalbl_cpk'  dose_df <- tibble::tibble(id = 1L, time = 0, amt = 22.5, regimen = "0.3 mg/kg single dose")  conc_obj <- PKNCA::PKNCAconc(sim_sd |> dplyr::select(id, time, Cc, regimen),                              Cc ~ time | regimen + id,                              concu = "mg/L", timeu = "hr") dose_obj <- PKNCA::PKNCAdose(dose_df, amt ~ time | regimen + id, doseu = "mg")  intervals <- data.frame(   start    = 0,   end      = 336,   cmax     = TRUE,   tmax     = TRUE,   auclast  = TRUE,   half.life = TRUE ) nca_res <- PKNCA::pk.nca(PKNCA::PKNCAdata(conc_obj, dose_obj, intervals = intervals)) nca_res$result |>   dplyr::select(start, end, PPTESTCD, PPORRES) |>   knitr::kable(digits = 4,                caption = "Simulated single-dose 0.3 mg/kg axatilimab NCA, typical cGVHD patient.")

title: Axatilimab semimechanistic PK/PD model (Yang 2024)
text: Mechanism-specific compartment names. biomarker states csf1, ncmc, ast, cpk deliberately deviate canonical compartment-name list naming-conventions.md (depot, central, peripheral1, etc.). chosen match variable names Yang 2024 Eqs. 5, 8, 10, 11 supplemental MLXTRAN code; using canonical names obscure one--one mapping published equations. nlmixr2lib::checkModelConventions() flags warnings (errors). Initial conditions. four biomarker states (csf1, ncmc, ast, cpk) set per-individual baseline parameter values (BL_CSF1, BL_NCMC, BL_AST, BL_CPK). PK compartments start zero (endogenous axatilimab). Saturable-elimination unit reformulation (mg vs μg). supplemental MLXTRAN code carries Ac μg converts dose 1000× factor iv() macro. nlmixr2lib model file works mg directly user-facing event tables can use mg without conversion; Vmax × C1 × vc × MW_PK / 1000 term mathematically equivalent Yang 2024 Eq. 1 (Errata note 3). kada interpretation. internal contradiction documented Errata note 1 resolved using value consistent Table 1, MLXTRAN supplement, formal covariate-effect summary (kada = 0.489). CSF-1 covariate units. Pg/mL (Figure 3’s mislabeled “ng/mL”); see Errata note 2. FDR / lower-LOQ handling. analysis dataset uses M4 method handle BLOQ samples (Beal 2001); since vignette runs deterministic typical-value simulations, BLOQ logic needed. PKNCA stratification. single regimen (0.3 mg/kg single dose) shown; published article report per-regimen NCA, cross-comparison paper values possible. Reference-value documentation. Continuous covariates (WT, CSF1, CPK) use overall pooled-cohort medians Yang 2024 Table S3 (73.6 kg, 549 pg/mL, 63 U/L). paper separately report cohort-stratified medians; using overall median follows Yang 2024’s statement “continuous covariates adjusted population median.”

what: Guselkumab population PK in moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis
title: Yao_2018_guselkumab
text: Simulate guselkumab serum concentration-time profiles using original population PK model Yao et al. (2018), built pooled phase 2 (X-PLORE) phase 3 (VOYAGE 1, VOYAGE 2) data adults moderate severe plaque psoriasis. Guselkumab fully human IgG1-lambda monoclonal antibody targeting p19 subunit interleukin-23. structural model one-compartment linear PK model first-order subcutaneous absorption first-order elimination. final reduced covariate set body weight apparent clearance (CL/F) apparent volume distribution (V/F), diabetes-mellitus race (non-White vs White) indicators CL/F. Typical parameters 87.1 kg non-diabetic White reference subject CL/F = 0.516 L/day, V/F = 13.5 L, Ka = 1.11 1/day, give typical terminal half-life approximately 18.1 days. Citation: Yao Z, Hu C, Zhu Y, Xu Z, Randazzo B, Wasfi Y, Chen Y, Sharma , Zhou H. Population Pharmacokinetic Modeling Guselkumab, Human IgG1-lambda Monoclonal Antibody Targeting IL-23, Patients Moderate Severe Plaque Psoriasis. J Clin Pharmacol. 2018;58(5):613-627. doi:10.1002/jcph.1063 Article: https://doi.org/10.1002/jcph.1063

what: Comparison against the related Chen 2022 popPK model
title: Yao_2018_guselkumab
text: package also includes Chen_2022_guselkumab, fits guselkumab population PK psoriatic arthritis (DISCOVER-1 / DISCOVER-2 phase 3 trials). models share one-compartment first-order SC absorption / first-order elimination structural form combined additive + proportional residual error structure. differ analysis dataset, reference covariates, retained covariate set: Despite different patient populations reference covariates, models converge essentially typical terminal half-life, consistent guselkumab’s IgG1 PK dictated FcRn-mediated recycling comparable demographic mix.

title: Yao_2018_guselkumab
text: Per-parameter origins recorded -file comments model file; table collects one place. footnote IIV Yao 2018 Table 4 reads “interindividual variability calculated (variance)^(1/2) x 100%”, .e., reported percentages standard deviation log-space (omega) times 100 rather linear-scale CV. Conversion therefore omega^2 = (IIV/100)^2 directly, omega^2 = log(1 + CV^2) form used papers report linear-scale CV (compare Chen_2022_guselkumab.R).

previous_headings: Guselkumab population PK in moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis
title: Yao_2018_guselkumab
text: Per Yao 2018 Tables 2 3 Patient Disposition Baseline Characteristics section: analysis pooled data X-PLORE (NCT01483599; phase 2 dose-ranging study, 238 patients), VOYAGE 1 (NCT02207231; phase 3, 492 patients), VOYAGE 2 (NCT02207244; phase 3, 724 patients), adults moderate--severe plaque psoriasis (1454 patients total, 13,014 PK records). Patient ages ranged 18 82 years (median 44 years, IQR 34-53), body weight ranged 45 198 kg (median 87.1 kg, IQR 74.8-100). population 70.8% male 29.2% female, race distribution 83.8% White, 12.2% Asian, 1.8% Black, 2.3% . Diabetes prevalence 8.9% ADA-positive prevalence 5.4%. phase 3 trials used uniform dosing regimen 100 mg SC weeks 0 4 followed every 8 weeks; X-PLORE used wider dose-ranging regimen (5-200 mg) varying schedules week 40. metadata available programmatically:
code: readModelDb("Yao_2018_guselkumab")$meta$population

title: Yao_2018_guselkumab
text: virtual cohort approximates demographics Yao 2018 Tables 2 3. Body weight sampled log-normal centered population median (87.1 kg). Diabetes sampled Bernoulli reported 8.9% prevalence. Race sampled Bernoulli (16.2% non-White) match pooled Asian + Black + percentage. cohort sized 500 subjects per regimen / dose group make per-time-point quantiles smooth.
code: set.seed(2018) n_subj <- 500L  pop <- tibble(   ID         = seq_len(n_subj),   WT         = pmin(pmax(rlnorm(n_subj, log(87.1), 0.20), 45), 198),  # kg, clipped to Yao 2018 reported range   DIAB       = as.integer(rbinom(n_subj, 1, 0.089)),                   # ~8.9% diabetic per Table 3   RACE_WHITE = as.integer(rbinom(n_subj, 1, 0.838))                    # 83.8% White per Table 3 )

previous_headings: Guselkumab population PK in moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis
title: Yao_2018_guselkumab
text: phase 3 approved clinical regimen simulated: q8w (approved): 100 mg SC weeks 0 4, every 8 weeks (weeks 12, 20, 28, …), week 52 reach approximate steady state. depot compartment SC dosing compartment (cmt = 1); central compartment sampling compartment (cmt = 2).
code: weeks_q8w     <- c(0, 4, seq(12, 52, by = 8))    # 0, 4, 12, 20, 28, 36, 44, 52 dose_times_q8w <- weeks_q8w * 7  obs_times <- sort(unique(c(   seq(0,   28,    by = 1),     # daily through week 4   seq(28, 52 * 7, by = 7)      # weekly through week 52 )))  make_events <- function(pop_df, dose_times, regimen, id_offset = 0L) {   pop_df <- pop_df %>% mutate(ID = ID + id_offset, regimen = regimen)   d_dose <- pop_df %>%     crossing(TIME = dose_times) %>%     mutate(AMT = 100, EVID = 1, CMT = 1, DV = NA_real_)   d_obs <- pop_df %>%     crossing(TIME = obs_times) %>%     mutate(AMT = NA_real_, EVID = 0, CMT = 2, DV = NA_real_)   bind_rows(d_dose, d_obs) %>%     arrange(ID, TIME, desc(EVID)) %>%     as.data.frame() }  events_q8w <- make_events(pop, dose_times_q8w, "q8w", id_offset = 0L)  events <- events_q8w stopifnot(!anyDuplicated(unique(events[, c("ID", "TIME", "EVID")])))

title: Yao_2018_guselkumab
text: Median 90% prediction interval simulated serum guselkumab concentration time course q8w regimen (replicates shape population PK simulation Figure 3 Yao 2018, top panels).
code: sim_summary <- sim %>%   filter(time > 0) %>%   group_by(regimen, time) %>%   summarise(     median = median(Cc, na.rm = TRUE),     lo     = quantile(Cc, 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),     hi     = quantile(Cc, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   )  ggplot(sim_summary, aes(x = time / 7, colour = regimen, fill = regimen)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lo, ymax = hi), alpha = 0.2, colour = NA) +   geom_line(aes(y = median), linewidth = 1) +   scale_y_log10() +   labs(     x        = "Time (weeks)",     y        = "Guselkumab concentration (ug/mL)",     title    = "Simulated guselkumab PK in plaque psoriasis (Yao 2018 model)",     subtitle = "Median and 90% prediction interval (N = 500 virtual patients, q8w)",     caption  = "Replicates the shape of Figure 3 (VPC) of Yao 2018."   ) +   theme_bw()

previous_headings: Guselkumab population PK in moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis
title: Yao_2018_guselkumab
text: Results section reports typical-subject CL/F = 0.516 L/day, V/F = 13.5 L, Ka = 1.11 1/day, typical terminal half-life approximately 18.1 days median 87.1 kg diabetes, White (Yao 2018 Results, page 619 Table 4). Reproduce using packaged model -subject variability zeroed .
code:
mod_typ <- rxode2::zeroRe(mod) #> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()'  typ_pop <- tibble(   ID         = 1L,   WT         = 87.1,   DIAB       = 0L,   RACE_WHITE = 1L )  typ_dose <- typ_pop %>%   crossing(TIME = dose_times_q8w) %>%   mutate(AMT = 100, EVID = 1, CMT = 1, DV = NA_real_)  typ_obs <- typ_pop %>%   crossing(TIME = obs_times) %>%   mutate(AMT = NA_real_, EVID = 0, CMT = 2, DV = NA_real_)  typ_events <- bind_rows(typ_dose, typ_obs) %>%   arrange(TIME, desc(EVID)) %>%   as.data.frame()  sim_typ <- rxSolve(mod_typ, typ_events, returnType = "data.frame") #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalcl', 'etalvc', 'etalka'  cl_typ <- 0.516 v_typ  <- 13.5 ka_typ <- 1.11 t_half <- log(2) * v_typ / cl_typ  cat(sprintf(   paste0(     "Typical 87.1 kg non-diabetic White subject (vs paper Table 4 / Results page 619):
",     "  CL/F = %.3f L/day  (paper: 0.516)
",     "  V/F  = %.2f L      (paper: 13.5)
",     "  Ka   = %.3f 1/day  (paper: 1.11)
",     "  t1/2 (typical, ln(2) * V/CL) = %.1f days  (paper: ~18.1)
"   ),   cl_typ, v_typ, ka_typ, t_half )) #> Typical 87.1 kg non-diabetic White subject (vs paper Table 4 / Results page 619): #>   CL/F = 0.516 L/day  (paper: 0.516) #>   V/F  = 13.50 L      (paper: 13.5) #>   Ka   = 1.110 1/day  (paper: 1.11) #>   t1/2 (typical, ln(2) * V/CL) = 18.1 days  (paper: ~18.1)  ggplot(sim_typ %>% filter(time > 0), aes(time / 7, Cc)) +   geom_line(colour = "darkred", linewidth = 1) +   scale_y_log10() +   labs(     x        = "Time (weeks)",     y        = "Guselkumab concentration (ug/mL)",     title    = "Typical 87.1 kg non-diabetic White subject, q8w (zeroed random effects)",     caption  = "100 mg SC at weeks 0 and 4, then every 8 weeks."   ) +   theme_bw()

title: Yao_2018_guselkumab
text: Run PKNCA steady-state q8w maintenance interval (weeks 44-52). expected typical terminal half-life ~18.1 days typical 87.1 kg White non-diabetic subject (Yao 2018 Results, page 619). PKNCA summary q8w steady-state maintenance interval (weeks 44-52). Expected typical terminal half-life ~18.1 days (Yao 2018 Results, page 619).
code: nca_conc <- sim %>%   filter(regimen == "q8w", time >= 44 * 7, time <= 52 * 7, Cc > 0) %>%   mutate(time_rel = time - 44 * 7) %>%   rename(ID = id) %>%   select(ID, time_rel, Cc, regimen)  nca_dose <- pop %>%   mutate(     time_rel = 0,     AMT      = 100,     regimen  = "q8w"   ) %>%   select(ID, time_rel, AMT, regimen)  conc_obj <- PKNCAconc(   nca_conc,   Cc ~ time_rel | regimen + ID,   concu = "ug/mL",   timeu = "day" ) dose_obj <- PKNCAdose(   nca_dose,   AMT ~ time_rel | regimen + ID,   doseu = "mg" ) data_obj <- PKNCAdata(   conc_obj,   dose_obj,   intervals = data.frame(     start     = 0,     end       = 8 * 7,     cmax      = TRUE,     tmax      = TRUE,     cmin      = TRUE,     auclast   = TRUE,     half.life = TRUE   ) ) nca_results <- pk.nca(data_obj) #>  ■■■■■■■■■■■                       34% |  ETA:  6s #>  ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■           75% |  ETA:  2s nca_summary <- summary(nca_results) knitr::kable(   nca_summary,   caption = "PKNCA summary for the q8w steady-state maintenance interval (weeks 44-52). Expected typical terminal half-life ~18.1 days (Yao 2018 Results, page 619)." )

what: Comparison against published Table 5 (steady-state q8w simulations)
title: Yao_2018_guselkumab
text: Yao 2018 Table 5 reports model-predicted median Ctrough,ss AUC weeks 0-8 steady state (AUC week0-8,ss) subgroups defined body weight, diabetes, race, simulated 5000 patients sampled original analysis dataset. Reproduce per-subgroup medians present 500-subject virtual cohort compare. Differences expected within sampling error smaller cohort . Comparison simulated steady-state q8w medians (cohort, N=500) Yao 2018 Table 5 (published medians paper’s 5000-subject simulation). AUC weeks 0-8 steady state ug*day/mL; Ctrough ug/mL. simulated subgroup medians use 500-subject virtual cohort sampled parametric covariate distributions, Yao 2018 Table 5 samples 5000 subjects original analysis dataset (covariate joint distribution differs slightly, especially weight-correlated covariates like diabetes). Within-cohort median--median differences <~ 20% indicate structural covariate model faithfully reproduced.
code: # Per-subject steady-state summary on the q8w maintenance interval (weeks 44-52) ss <- sim %>%   filter(regimen == "q8w", time >= 44 * 7, time <= 52 * 7, Cc > 0) %>%   arrange(id, time)  per_id <- ss %>%   group_by(id, WT, DIAB, RACE_WHITE) %>%   summarise(     Ctrough = Cc[which.max(time)],     AUCtau  = sum(diff(time) * (head(Cc, -1) + tail(Cc, -1)) / 2),     .groups = "drop"   )  subgroup_medians <- bind_rows(   per_id %>% filter(WT < 90)      %>% summarise(group = "Weight < 90 kg",    n = n(),                                                 Median_AUC     = median(AUCtau),                                                 Median_Ctrough = median(Ctrough)),   per_id %>% filter(WT >= 90)     %>% summarise(group = "Weight >= 90 kg",   n = n(),                                                 Median_AUC     = median(AUCtau),                                                 Median_Ctrough = median(Ctrough)),   per_id %>% filter(DIAB == 0)    %>% summarise(group = "No diabetes",       n = n(),                                                 Median_AUC     = median(AUCtau),                                                 Median_Ctrough = median(Ctrough)),   per_id %>% filter(DIAB == 1)    %>% summarise(group = "Diabetes",          n = n(),                                                 Median_AUC     = median(AUCtau),                                                 Median_Ctrough = median(Ctrough)),   per_id %>% filter(RACE_WHITE == 1) %>% summarise(group = "White",          n = n(),                                                    Median_AUC     = median(AUCtau),                                                    Median_Ctrough = median(Ctrough)),   per_id %>% filter(RACE_WHITE == 0) %>% summarise(group = "Non-White",      n = n(),                                                    Median_AUC     = median(AUCtau),                                                    Median_Ctrough = median(Ctrough)) )  # Yao 2018 Table 5 published medians for AUC_week0-8,ss (ug*day/mL) and Ctrough,ss (ug/mL) published <- tibble(   group              = c("Weight < 90 kg", "Weight >= 90 kg",                          "No diabetes",    "Diabetes",                          "White",          "Non-White"),   Published_AUC      = c(225, 159, 196, 158, 192, 192),   Published_Ctrough  = c(1.21, 0.80, 1.04, 0.72, 1.02, 0.93) )  comparison <- subgroup_medians %>%   left_join(published, by = "group") %>%   mutate(     Pct_diff_AUC     = 100 * (Median_AUC     - Published_AUC)     / Published_AUC,     Pct_diff_Ctrough = 100 * (Median_Ctrough - Published_Ctrough) / Published_Ctrough   )  knitr::kable(   comparison,   digits  = 2,   caption = "Comparison of simulated steady-state q8w medians (this cohort, N=500) against Yao 2018 Table 5 (published medians from the paper's 5000-subject simulation). AUC is over weeks 0-8 at steady state in ug*day/mL; Ctrough is in ug/mL." )

previous_headings: Guselkumab population PK in moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis
what: Covariate-effect simulation: heavier (>= 90 kg), diabetic, and non-White subgroups
title: Yao_2018_guselkumab
text: Yao 2018 reports (Results, Simulation; Table 5) q8w regimen model predicts: patients body weight >= 90 kg vs. < 90 kg AUC lower roughly 29% Ctrough lower roughly 34%; patients vs. without diabetes AUC lower roughly 19% Ctrough lower roughly 31%; non-White vs White subjects show comparable AUC, small Ctrough difference. Reproduce contrasts using zeroed random effects (typical-subject predictions) q8w steady state (weeks 44-52). body-weight contrast uses 95 kg vs. 80 kg round-number representatives straddle 90 kg cutoff used paper’s subgroup definitions. Steady-state q8w typical-subject covariate-effect contrasts (Ctrough ug/mL; AUCtau ug*day/mL). Compare Yao 2018 Table 5 / Results: heavier patients ~34% lower Ctrough ~29% lower AUC; diabetic patients ~31% lower Ctrough ~19% lower AUC. body-weight contrast simulated 95 vs. 80 kg (typical-subject anchors straddling 90 kg cutoff) rather median---subgroup paper used report Table 5’s subgroup medians, within---percent difference expected. diabetes race contrasts fixed 80 kg reproduce paper’s multipliers (1.12 DIAB, 1.11 non-White CL/F) closely operate independently body weight.
code: typ_eval <- function(WT_val, DIAB_val, RW_val) {   pop1   <- tibble(ID = 1L, WT = WT_val, DIAB = as.integer(DIAB_val),                    RACE_WHITE = as.integer(RW_val))   d_dose <- pop1 %>% crossing(TIME = dose_times_q8w) %>%     mutate(AMT = 100, EVID = 1, CMT = 1, DV = NA_real_)   d_obs  <- pop1 %>% crossing(TIME = obs_times) %>%     mutate(AMT = NA_real_, EVID = 0, CMT = 2, DV = NA_real_)   ev <- bind_rows(d_dose, d_obs) %>% arrange(TIME, desc(EVID)) %>% as.data.frame()   s  <- rxSolve(mod_typ, ev, returnType = "data.frame") %>%     filter(time >= 44 * 7, time <= 52 * 7, Cc > 0) %>% arrange(time)   list(     Ctrough = tail(s$Cc, 1),     AUCtau  = sum(diff(s$time) * (head(s$Cc, -1) + tail(s$Cc, -1)) / 2)   ) }  ref_white <- typ_eval(80,  0, 1) #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalcl', 'etalvc', 'etalka' heavy95   <- typ_eval(95,  0, 1) #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalcl', 'etalvc', 'etalka' diab80    <- typ_eval(80,  1, 1) #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalcl', 'etalvc', 'etalka' nonwhite  <- typ_eval(80,  0, 0) #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalcl', 'etalvc', 'etalka'  pct <- function(new, ref) 100 * (new - ref) / ref  cov_table <- tibble(   contrast        = c("WT 95 vs 80 kg, no diabetes, White",                       "Diabetes vs no diabetes, 80 kg, White",                       "Non-White vs White, 80 kg, no diabetes"),   Ctrough_ref     = rep(ref_white$Ctrough, 3),   Ctrough_new     = c(heavy95$Ctrough, diab80$Ctrough, nonwhite$Ctrough),   Ctrough_pct_chg = c(pct(heavy95$Ctrough, ref_white$Ctrough),                       pct(diab80$Ctrough,  ref_white$Ctrough),                       pct(nonwhite$Ctrough, ref_white$Ctrough)),   AUCtau_ref      = rep(ref_white$AUCtau, 3),   AUCtau_new      = c(heavy95$AUCtau, diab80$AUCtau, nonwhite$AUCtau),   AUCtau_pct_chg  = c(pct(heavy95$AUCtau, ref_white$AUCtau),                       pct(diab80$AUCtau,  ref_white$AUCtau),                       pct(nonwhite$AUCtau, ref_white$AUCtau)) )  knitr::kable(   cov_table,   digits  = 2,   caption = "Steady-state q8w typical-subject covariate-effect contrasts (Ctrough in ug/mL; AUCtau in ug*day/mL). Compare to Yao 2018 Table 5 / Results: heavier patients have ~34% lower Ctrough and ~29% lower AUC; diabetic patients have ~31% lower Ctrough and ~19% lower AUC." )

title: Yao_2018_guselkumab
text: team performed search PubMed journal landing page (https://accp1.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/jcph.1063) errata, corrigenda, author corrections covering Yao 2018 found published corrections time model extraction. Several typesetting artifacts published PDF affect readability model values; recorded future readers mistake genuine notation: Greek omega rendered “/Omega1” / “/omega1” sentences describing IIV variance-covariance matrix (e.g., “vector random effects covariance matrix /Omega1”). intended symbol upper-case omega. Inequality Greek epsilon glyphs mangled places (e.g., “/22656” appears “>=”, “/223c” appears “approximately equal” “epsilon”). text reading unambiguous context. Words occasionally lack inter-word spaces (e.g., “T-helper(Th)cells”, “1-compartmentlinearPKmodel”). PDF text-extraction artifact, published article. None affect parameter values, equations Tables 4 5, residual-error structure extracted model file.

previous_headings: Guselkumab population PK in moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis
title: Yao_2018_guselkumab
text: Yao 2018 publish individual-level PK per-subject covariate values, virtual population approximates main-text demographics rather reproducing : Weight: sampled log-normal around 87.1 kg median (Table 2) SD 0.20 log scale, clipped 45-198 kg (reported range Patient Disposition). approximately matches reported 25th-75th percentile range (74.8-100 kg). Diabetes: sampled Bernoulli(0.089) (~8.9% prevalence per Table 3). Race: sampled Bernoulli(0.838) RACE_WHITE = 1 match 83.8% White proportion Table 3. non-White complement pools Asian (12.2%), Black (1.8%), (2.3%) per paper’s binary “Race (nonwhite)” covariate definition. Sex, age, ethnicity, ADA status, baseline laboratory values, prior biologics, smoking, alcohol, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, concomitant medications: evaluated paper’s full covariate model <10% effect CL/F V/F dropped final reduced model (Yao 2018 Results, Full Covariate Model Final Reduced Covariate Model sections; Figure 1). part packaged PK model. Bioavailability (F): absolute bioavailability separately identifiable SC-data; model uses apparent CL/F V/F published, depot compartment dosed directly. f(depot) term included. Residual error: combined add(addSd) + prop(propSd) form standard nlmixr2 implementation paper’s “combined additive proportional residual error model” (Methods, Base Model section), addSd ug/mL matching concentration units. additive component fixed Yao 2018 0.00289 ug/mL based uniform-distribution probability characteristic associated LLOQ 0.01 ug/mL; fixed value used . IIV interpretation: Yao 2018 reports “interindividual variability calculated (variance)^(1/2) x 100%” (Table 4 footnote). treats percentage SD log scale (omega) times 100, linear-scale CV. omega^2 used model therefore (IIV/100)^2 directly, log(1 + CV^2) form used monoclonal-antibody papers. ka IIV = 129%, interpretation makes large numerical difference; paper’s footnote followed verbatim. time-varying covariates. Body weight, diabetes status, race time-fixed baseline values; paper describe time-varying covariate effects. Bigger published simulation cohort. Yao 2018 Table 5 simulations resampled 5000 subjects original analysis dataset; validation block uses 500 simulated subjects parametric covariate distributions, sufficient reproduce subgroup median patterns exhibit sampling noise.

title: Yao_2018_guselkumab
text: Yao Z, Hu C, Zhu Y, Xu Z, Randazzo B, Wasfi Y, Chen Y, Sharma , Zhou H. Population Pharmacokinetic Modeling Guselkumab, Human IgG1-lambda Monoclonal Antibody Targeting IL-23, Patients Moderate Severe Plaque Psoriasis. J Clin Pharmacol. 2018;58(5):613-627. doi:10.1002/jcph.1063

title: Yin_2021_trastuzumabDeruxtecan
text: Citation: Yin O, Iwata H, Lin C-C, Tamura K, Watanabe J, Wada R, Kastrissios H, Garimella T, Lee C, Zhang L, Shahidi J, Fujisaki Y, LaCreta F. Population Pharmacokinetics Trastuzumab Deruxtecan Patients HER2-Positive Breast Cancer Solid Tumors. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 2021;109(5):1314-1325. doi:[10.1002/cpt.2096](https://doi.org/10.1002/cpt.2096) Article: https://doi.org/10.1002/cpt.2096 Description: Two-compartment population PK model intact trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd, DS-8201, anti-HER2 antibody-drug conjugate) linear elimination covariate effects body weight, albumin, baseline tumor size, sex, Japan-country indicator patients HER2-positive breast cancer HER2-expressing solid tumors. Modality: Antibody-drug conjugate (humanized IgG1 anti-HER2 backbone, cleavable peptide linker, topoisomerase--inhibitor payload, drug-- antibody ratio ~8). IV infusion, 0.8-8.0 mg/kg every 3 weeks. Trastuzumab deruxtecan FDA-approved adult patients HER2-positive unresectable / metastatic breast cancer received two prior anti-HER2 regimens; approved dose used characterize steady-state exposure Yin 2021 5.4 mg/kg every 3 weeks. packaged model encodes intact T-DXd PK; released-drug (DXd payload) compartment described paper implemented (see Assumptions deviations). Structure: linear two-compartment IV model first-order elimination central compartment. CLi,intact=0.421⋅(WT/57.8)0.370⋅(ALB/40)−0.533⋅(TUMSZ/57)0.0710⋅(1−0.0970⋅COUNTRY_JPN)⋅(1+0.174⋅(1−SEXF))⋅eηCL \mathrm{CL}_{,\text{intact}} =   0.421   \cdot (\mathrm{WT}    / 57.8)^{0.370}   \cdot (\mathrm{ALB}   / 40)^{-0.533}   \cdot (\mathrm{TUMSZ} / 57)^{0.0710}   \cdot (1 - 0.0970 \cdot \mathrm{COUNTRY\_JPN})   \cdot (1 + 0.174   \cdot (1 - \mathrm{SEXF}))   \cdot e^{\eta_{\mathrm{CL}}} V1,,intact=2.77⋅(WT/57.8)0.489⋅(1+0.197⋅(1−SEXF))⋅eηV1 \mathrm{V}_{1,,\text{intact}} =   2.77   \cdot (\mathrm{WT} / 57.8)^{0.489}   \cdot (1 + 0.197 \cdot (1 - \mathrm{SEXF}))   \cdot e^{\eta_{V_1}} V2,,intact=5.16⋅(1−0.262⋅COUNTRY_JPN)⋅eηV2,Qi,intact=0.199⋅eηQ \mathrm{V}_{2,,\text{intact}} =   5.16   \cdot (1 - 0.262 \cdot \mathrm{COUNTRY\_JPN})   \cdot e^{\eta_{V_2}}, \qquad \mathrm{Q}_{,\text{intact}} = 0.199 \cdot e^{\eta_Q}

title: Yin_2021_trastuzumabDeruxtecan
text: final-model population comprised 639 patients pooled across 5 trials (Yin 2021 Methods Analysis data set sampling schedules, Table S1): 4 phase studies: J101, J102, A103, A104 (last two Japanese phase US drug-drug-interaction studies, respectively). 1 phase II study: DESTINY-Breast01 (HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer previously treated T-DM1). Dose range 0.8 - 8.0 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (q3w), 21-day treatment cycles. Approved breast-cancer dose: 5.4 mg/kg q3w. Disease demographics: 512 / 639 (80.1%) unresectable / metastatic breast cancer; 445 / 639 (69.6%) HER2-positive unresectable / metastatic breast cancer. tumor types include HER2-expressing gastric, lung, colorectal, salivary-gland, solid tumors. reference patient (Figure 4 caption) female, non-Japan country, 57.8 kg, albumin 40 g/L, baseline tumor size 57 mm. cohort spans Japan non-Japan countries; race country highly confounded (correlation -0.81), authors retained country final model. 5,401 / 11,434 (47.2%) intact T-DXd serum concentrations contributed final analysis. metadata available programmatically via readModelDb("Yin_2021_trastuzumabDeruxtecan")$population.

title: Yin_2021_trastuzumabDeruxtecan
text: per-parameter origin recorded -file comment next ini() entry inst/modeldb/specificDrugs/Yin_2021_trastuzumabDeruxtecan.R. table collects one place review. Equations: structural two-compartment micro-constant form linear elimination central compartment. Continuous covariates enter (cov / ref)^exponent; categorical covariates enter (1 + theta * indicator). Reference covariates (Yin 2021 Figure 4 caption): female, non-Japan country, 57.8 kg, 40 g/L albumin, 57 mm baseline tumor size.

title: Yin_2021_trastuzumabDeruxtecan
text: Original observed data publicly available. simulations use virtual cohort whose demographics approximate pooled Yin 2021 population reference covariate values (Figure 4 caption). Continuous covariates drawn log-normal distributions anchored reported median; binary / categorical covariates match approximate marginal proportions implied DESTINY-Breast01 + Japanese phase cohort mix. single dosing regimen simulated: 5.4 mg/kg q3w, approved breast-cancer dose used paper reference steady-state exposure comparisons. label tag rides rxSolve via keep = PKNCA can stratify treatment.
code: set.seed(2021) n_subj <- 200  cohort <- tibble::tibble(   ID          = seq_len(n_subj),   WT          = pmin(pmax(rlnorm(n_subj, log(57.8), 0.20), 35), 110),   ALB         = pmin(pmax(rnorm(n_subj, 40,    3.5), 28), 50),   TUMSZ       = pmin(pmax(rlnorm(n_subj, log(57),  0.50),  5), 250),   SEXF        = rbinom(n_subj, 1, 0.94),  # ~94% female (breast-cancer dominated)   REGION_JAPAN = rbinom(n_subj, 1, 0.30)   # roughly Japanese-phase-I share of total ) dose_interval_d <- 21 n_doses         <- 10 dose_times_d    <- seq(0, by = dose_interval_d, length.out = n_doses) sim_horizon_d   <- dose_times_d[n_doses] + dose_interval_d obs_times_d     <- sort(unique(c(dose_times_d,                                  seq(0, sim_horizon_d, by = 1))))  build_events <- function(pop, mgkg) {   amt_per_subject <- pop$WT * mgkg   d_dose <- pop |>     dplyr::mutate(AMT = amt_per_subject) |>     tidyr::crossing(TIME = dose_times_d) |>     dplyr::mutate(EVID = 1, CMT = "central", DUR = 30 / 60 / 24,  # 30-min IV infusion                   DV = NA_real_,                   treatment = paste0(mgkg, " mg/kg q3w"))   d_obs <- pop |>     tidyr::crossing(TIME = obs_times_d) |>     dplyr::mutate(AMT = NA_real_, EVID = 0, CMT = "central",                   DUR = NA_real_, DV = NA_real_,                   treatment = paste0(mgkg, " mg/kg q3w"))   dplyr::bind_rows(d_dose, d_obs) |>     dplyr::arrange(ID, TIME, dplyr::desc(EVID)) |>     as.data.frame() }  events <- build_events(cohort, 5.4)

title: Yin_2021_trastuzumabDeruxtecan
code: mod <- readModelDb("Yin_2021_trastuzumabDeruxtecan") sim <- rxode2::rxSolve(mod, events = events,                        keep = c("treatment"),                        returnType = "data.frame") #> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()'

title: Yin_2021_trastuzumabDeruxtecan
text: Yin 2021 Figure 1 (panels -c) shows observed concentration-time profiles study dose level first 504 hours (= 21 days = one dosing interval) first dose. figure renders simulated median 5-95% prediction interval 5.4 mg/kg q3w arm 10 dosing cycles.
code: sim_summary <- sim |>   dplyr::filter(time > 0) |>   dplyr::group_by(time, treatment) |>   dplyr::summarise(     median = stats::median(Cc, na.rm = TRUE),     lo     = stats::quantile(Cc, 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),     hi     = stats::quantile(Cc, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   )  ggplot2::ggplot(sim_summary, ggplot2::aes(time, median)) +   ggplot2::geom_ribbon(ggplot2::aes(ymin = lo, ymax = hi),                        alpha = 0.2, fill = "steelblue") +   ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 1, colour = "steelblue") +   ggplot2::scale_y_log10() +   ggplot2::labs(     x = "Time since first dose (days)",     y = "Intact T-DXd concentration (ug/mL, log scale)",     title = "Simulated intact T-DXd PK at 5.4 mg/kg q3w",     subtitle = paste0("Median and 90% prediction interval (N = ",                       n_subj, " virtual patients)."),     caption = "Yin 2021 model. Compare to Figure 1a-c (observed PK profiles)."   ) +   ggplot2::theme_bw()

what: Forest plot at the reference subject
title: Yin_2021_trastuzumabDeruxtecan
text: Yin 2021 Figure 4 reports covariate-effect forest plot typical reference patient (female, non-Japan, 57.8 kg, 40 g/L albumin, 57 mm tumor size) using 5.4 mg/kg q3w regimen. following deterministic check (etas zeroed) reproduces typical parameter values reference covariates, perturbs covariate 5th 95th percentile visualize single-covariate effect steady-state CL_intact:  5th-percentile body weight (39 kg) 95th-percentile body weight (86 kg) drive largest single-covariate excursions CL_intact, exactly Figure 4 reports AUC Cmin. albumin--CL effect (5th percentile albumin 31 g/L corresponds ~17% increase CL typical-value plot) second-largest shifter, matching paper’s narrative.
code: ref <- list(WT = 57.8, ALB = 40, TUMSZ = 57, SEXF = 1, REGION_JAPAN = 0)  # Yin 2021 Figure 4 covariate ranges (5th and 95th percentile values # of the analysis dataset, as cited by the paper text). cov_grid <- tibble::tribble(   ~covariate,    ~level,        ~WT,   ~ALB, ~TUMSZ, ~SEXF, ~REGION_JAPAN,   "Reference",   "Reference",   57.8,  40,   57,     1,     0,   "WT 5th",      "39 kg",       39,    40,   57,     1,     0,   "WT 95th",     "86 kg",       86,    40,   57,     1,     0,   "ALB 5th",     "31 g/L",      57.8,  31,   57,     1,     0,   "ALB 95th",    "47 g/L",      57.8,  47,   57,     1,     0,   "TUMSZ 5th",   "10 mm",       57.8,  40,   10,     1,     0,   "TUMSZ 95th",  "150 mm",      57.8,  40,  150,     1,     0,   "Sex male",    "Male",        57.8,  40,   57,     0,     0,   "Country JPN", "Japan",       57.8,  40,   57,     1,     1 )  # Closed-form CL_intact at the typical (eta = 0) value for each row. cov_grid$cl_typical <- with(cov_grid,   0.421 *     (WT    / 57.8)^0.370 *     (ALB   / 40  )^-0.533 *     (TUMSZ / 57  )^0.0710 *     (1 + (-0.0970) * REGION_JAPAN) *     (1 + 0.174 * (1 - SEXF)) )  cov_grid$ratio <- cov_grid$cl_typical / cov_grid$cl_typical[1]  ggplot2::ggplot(cov_grid[-1, ],                 ggplot2::aes(x = ratio,                              y = stats::reorder(level, ratio))) +   ggplot2::geom_point(size = 2.5, colour = "steelblue") +   ggplot2::geom_vline(xintercept = c(0.8, 1.0, 1.25),                       linetype = c("dashed", "solid", "dashed"),                       colour = "grey50") +   ggplot2::labs(     x = "CL_intact ratio (covariate-perturbed vs reference)",     y = NULL,     title = "Univariate covariate effects on typical CL_intact",     subtitle = "Reference: female, non-Japan, 57.8 kg, 40 g/L albumin, 57 mm tumor size",     caption = "Compare to Yin 2021 Figure 4 (covariate forest plot at 5.4 mg/kg q3w)."   ) +   ggplot2::theme_bw()

title: Yin_2021_trastuzumabDeruxtecan
text: Compute steady-state NCA parameters 10th (last simulated) dosing interval 5.4 mg/kg q3w. Yin 2021 reports model-derived geometric-mean steady-state exposures (Figure 4 reference subject) AUC_tau,ss ~ 735 ug.day/mL, Cmax,ss ~ 122 ug/mL, Cmin,ss ~ 9.7 ug/mL reference patient (read y-axis Figure 4’s “Reference” row 5.4 mg/kg q3w; paper publish dedicated per-cohort NCA table). PKNCA summary within-simulation consistency check. Simulated NCA parameters steady state (10th dosing interval, days 189-210, 5.4 mg/kg q3w).
code: interval_start <- dose_times_d[n_doses] interval_end   <- interval_start + dose_interval_d  sim_nca <- sim |>   dplyr::filter(!is.na(Cc),                 time >= interval_start,                 time <= interval_end) |>   dplyr::mutate(time_rel = time - interval_start) |>   dplyr::select(id, treatment, time_rel, Cc)  conc_obj <- PKNCA::PKNCAconc(sim_nca, Cc ~ time_rel | treatment + id,                              concu = "ug/mL", timeu = "day")  dose_df <- sim |>   dplyr::filter(time == interval_start) |>   dplyr::group_by(id, treatment) |>   dplyr::summarise(.groups = "drop") |>   dplyr::left_join(cohort |> dplyr::select(id = ID, WT), by = "id") |>   dplyr::mutate(amt = WT * 5.4, time_rel = 0) |>   dplyr::select(id, treatment, time_rel, amt)  dose_obj <- PKNCA::PKNCAdose(dose_df, amt ~ time_rel | treatment + id,                              doseu = "mg")  intervals <- data.frame(   start    = 0,   end      = dose_interval_d,   cmax     = TRUE,   tmax     = TRUE,   cmin     = TRUE,   auclast  = TRUE,   cav      = TRUE )  nca_data <- PKNCA::PKNCAdata(conc_obj, dose_obj, intervals = intervals) nca_res  <- PKNCA::pk.nca(nca_data) knitr::kable(   summary(nca_res),   caption = "Simulated NCA parameters at steady state (10th dosing interval, days 189-210, 5.4 mg/kg q3w)." )
